lines: Fog rolls off the gutters and pools around a crooked lamppost where the road forks. One branch climbs to the bell tower, the other slides into shadow that seems to breathe. A pale shape steps from the mist, cloak dragging like a torn wing. The Nightbringer has picked its hunting ground. Type quick before it picks you.

Rain slicks the rooftops above the first branch, where loose tiles slide and gutters spill into the dark below. Something perches on a crooked chimney, watching with eyes like dying embers. It drops without a sound, cloak snapping open like leather wings. Race the clock before those fangs find your throat.

The lamplighter never reached this corner tonight; his ladder lies broken against the wall. In his place waits a tall shape in a fine coat, gloved hands folded, patient as frost. The Nightbringer bows low, mocking, before its smile splits far too wide. Strike true before the courtesy ends.

Fog thickens at the fork where three narrow streets meet beneath a dead streetlamp. A door creaks somewhere in the dark, though no hand opens it. A pale shape steps from between two shuttered stalls, cloak dragging through spilled rainwater, eyes catching the last light like a coin. Fight now.

A crooked signpost marks the branch in the road, one arm pointing to the guild hall, the other snapped clean off. Mist pools ankle deep and swallows the sound of your own footsteps. Something tall unfolds from a doorway, cloak whispering against brick. The Nightbringer has been waiting all along.

Gutters overflow along the alley where the road splits in two, carrying dead leaves and a single dropped glove past your boots. A shape detaches from the fog, taller than any door frame should allow. Its smile is handsome and wrong all at once. Type the line before it steps any closer.

The streetlamp gutters and dies at the exact spot where the alley forks, plunging the crooked lane into full dark. Somewhere close a cloak drags across wet cobbles, slow and unhurried. A pale hand beckons from the black doorway ahead. The Nightbringer never needs to hurry when its prey is trapped.

Sewer grates rattle underfoot where the tunnel branches beneath the tenements, one way to the docks, the other to nowhere good. A cold wind carries the smell of old blood and older stone. Something with too many teeth smiles from the dark fork ahead. Type fast before the smile finishes forming.

Rooftop tiles clatter loose where the chimneys crowd together above the fork in the street below. A shape crouches there against the fog, cloak pooling like spilled ink around gloved hands. It tips its head, listening for your breath. The Nightbringer has all night, but you do not.

The bell tower creaks above a crooked branch of alleyway where the cobbles turn slick with old rain. A courier's dropped satchel lies split open, papers scattering in the wind. From the dark beyond it steps a tall figure, smiling like it already knows how this ends. Prove it wrong.

Lamplight gutters along the narrow fork where the guild district splits from the slums, one branch lit, the other black as a shut door. Something moves in that dark half, unhurried, patient, gloved fingers trailing along brick. The Nightbringer only ever chooses the darker branch. So did you.

Mist condensing on the cobbles turns the whole crooked street to a mirror of dim light. Where the road splits, a pale shape waits with its back to a shuttered window, cloak hanging still despite the wind. Its eyes open slow, catching the last streetlamp like flame. Type before it blinks again.

A dropped coin rolls to a stop at the fork in the alley, spinning once before it settles heads up. Fog swallows the sound of the second footstep that was never yours. A tall shape leans out from behind a rain barrel, smile widening like a wound. The Nightbringer collects more than coin tonight.

The tenement stairs branch into two crooked landings, one lit by a guttering candle, the other pitch dark and silent. Something in that dark landing exhales slowly, cold enough to fog the nearest window. A gloved hand rests on the broken railing. Climb fast, or let the Nightbringer climb first.

Rain drums on tin roofs where the alley forks past a boarded tavern, gutters roaring with runoff toward the sewer grate. A tall shape steps out unbothered by the storm, cloak dry despite the downpour. Its smile is far too handsome for the teeth behind it. Type quickly before it offers you shelter.

The lamplighter's ladder still leans where he left it, abandoned at the fork where the crooked street splits toward the docks. Fog curls around its rungs like fingers. A voice, quiet and courteous, offers a greeting from the dark beyond. The Nightbringer always sounds kind before it strikes.

Cobblestones glisten at the branch where three alleys meet under a cracked streetlamp, its light flickering weak and gold. A shape unfolds from the deepest shadow, taller than the doorway it stands beside. Gloved fingers curl in invitation. Answer with your own hands and win this fork.

A church bell tolls once as the road forks past the crumbling chapel, and the sound dies fast in the thick fog. Something pale crouches on the low wall, cloak pooling around its boots like spilled ink. It rises slow, smiling with a courtesy that chills more than the wind. Fight now.

The gutter runs black with old rain where the alley splits behind the tanner's shop, carrying a single ribbon downstream. A tall shape steps from the fork's darker branch, smile handsome, eyes hollow. It bows, mocking, gloved hand extended. The Nightbringer always offers the first move.

Fog rolls thick through the fork where the cobbler's row meets a dead end lit by one dying lamp. A shape detaches from the brick, cloak dragging soundless over wet stone. Its smile is quiet, its patience quieter still. Type your line before that patience finally runs out.

The crooked lane splits at an old well, one branch toward the guild hall, the other toward silence. Mist pools thick around the well's cracked stones. A tall figure rises from beside it, gloved hands folded politely, waiting. The Nightbringer offers you first choice of branch. Choose speed.

Loose shutters bang somewhere above the fork in the alley, rattled by a wind that smells faintly of copper. A pale shape steps from behind a rain barrel, cloak whispering against brick, smile widening slow. Its courtesy is a mask worn thin. Strike before the mask finally slips.

The lamplight flickers twice and dies at the branch where the market street narrows to a crooked throat of stone. Something waits just past the dark, patient as a held breath. A gloved hand beckons once from the shadow. The Nightbringer only needs you to take one more step.

Rain pools in the cracked cobbles where the road forks near the old bell tower, reflecting a streetlamp that gutters weak and gold. A tall shape steps into that reflection, cloak dragging soundless. Its smile is handsome enough to be believed. Do not believe it. Type fast.

A courier's cart sits abandoned at the fork, one wheel broken, papers scattered across wet cobbles. Fog swallows the street beyond it whole. Something tall steps from that fog, gloved hands folded, smile courteous and cold. The Nightbringer always finds the ones who linger.

The alley forks past a shuttered bakery, the smell of old bread lost beneath damp stone and colder air. A pale shape crouches on the sill above, cloak pooling like ink around its boots. It drops without a sound, smile already waiting. Race the clock before it lands.

Mist thickens where the crooked street splits near the tannery, carrying the smell of old blood no rain can wash clean. A tall figure steps from the fork's darker half, gloved hand extended in mock welcome. The Nightbringer bows low, smiling wide. Answer its bow with speed.

The gutter overflows at the fork behind the guild hall, carrying a dropped glove past your boots toward the drain. A shape unfolds from the shadow beside the drainpipe, taller than it should be, smile patient. Its eyes catch the last lamp like a struck match. Fight now.

A dead streetlamp marks the branch where the alley splits toward the docks, its glass long since broken by some other night's trouble. Fog pools thick and cold around your ankles. Something pale steps from the dark half of the fork, gloved hands folded, smiling slow. Type fast.

The crooked lane narrows to a single branch behind the chapel, walls pressing close on either side. A tall shape waits there already, cloak dry despite the drizzle, smile courteous and cold. It inclines its head once, patient as stone. The Nightbringer never needs to chase.

Rain hammers the tin roofs above the fork where two alleys meet near a boarded inn, gutters roaring toward the sewer grate below. A shape steps out unbothered by the storm, gloved hand rising in quiet greeting. The Nightbringer's smile is warmer than the weather. Do not trust it.

The lamplighter's route ends at this fork tonight, ladder abandoned against a crooked wall streaked with old rain. Fog curls thick around the dead lamp overhead. A tall figure steps from the dark beyond it, smile patient, gloved hand extended. Answer quickly, before the fog closes in.

Fog rolls off the gutters in slow grey sheets, pooling around a crooked lamppost at the exact spot where the alley splits in two. One branch climbs toward the bell tower and its promise of open sky. The other slides down into shadow that seems, impossibly, to breathe on its own. Somewhere close a cloak drags across wet cobbles, unhurried, patient, certain.

You choose your footing carefully, boots finding the dry stones between puddles, and that is when the shape steps free of the mist. It is taller than any doorway has a right to allow, gloved hands folded, smile far too handsome for what waits behind it. The Nightbringer has already chosen its branch tonight. Now it only needs you to choose wrong.

Rain slicks the rooftops above the first branch of the old quarter, where loose tiles slide underfoot and gutters spill silver rain down into the dark street below. You climb because the ground way is blocked, because a courier's overturned cart has spilled crates across the only door. Somewhere above, a shape crouches on a crooked chimney, watching with eyes like dying embers, waiting for you to look up.

It drops without a sound, cloak snapping open like leather wings catching wind. For one long heartbeat you see its whole shape against the fogged moon, elegant and wrong, before it lands soft as falling ash between you and the ladder home. Race the clock now, because the Nightbringer never falls twice.

The lamplighter never reached this corner tonight. His ladder lies broken against the crooked wall, rungs snapped clean through, and the lamp above it gutters weak and dying. In his place waits a tall shape in a fine coat, gloved hands folded with unsettling patience, the kind of stillness that only comes from something that has stopped needing breath.

It bows low when it notices you watching, mocking and courteous all at once, before its smile splits far too wide for any handsome face to hold. Somewhere behind it the fog thickens, swallowing the last dim light of the guttering lamp. The Nightbringer offers you the first move only because it already knows how the second one ends.

Fog thickens at the fork where three narrow streets meet beneath a dead streetlamp, its glass cracked from some earlier night's trouble. A door creaks somewhere close in the dark, though no hand ever opens it, and the sound repeats twice more before it stops. You steady your grip and step forward anyway, because the branch behind you is worse.

A pale shape detaches itself from between two shuttered market stalls, cloak dragging soundless through spilled rainwater. Its eyes catch the last thread of lamplight like a struck coin, bright and cold. The Nightbringer studies you a long moment, unhurried, before it finally decides you are worth the trouble of a proper fight.

A crooked signpost marks the branch in the road just past the tannery, one arm pointing toward the guild hall's warm lit windows, the other snapped clean off by some older storm. Mist pools ankle deep here and swallows the sound of your own footsteps entirely, so that you cannot tell if you walk alone.

Something tall unfolds itself from the black mouth of a doorway, cloak whispering against old brick like a held breath finally released. The Nightbringer has been waiting here a long while, patient as stone, gloved hand rising in a slow and terrible welcome. Type your line before that patience finally runs its course.

Gutters overflow along the tenement row where the alley splits into two uneven halves, carrying dead leaves and a single dropped glove past your boots toward the drain. Somewhere above, a shutter bangs loose in the wind, again and again, like a heartbeat that will not settle. You press on toward the darker branch because it is the only one that leads home.

A shape detaches from the fog there, taller than any door frame should allow, cloak pooling around gloved boots like spilled ink. Its smile is handsome and wrong all at once, courteous in a way that makes your skin go cold. The Nightbringer only ever waits this long for prey it considers worth the wait.

The streetlamp gutters twice and dies at the exact spot where the alley forks, plunging the whole crooked lane into a dark thick enough to taste. Somewhere close a cloak drags slow across wet cobbles, and you count the seconds between each footstep, though you know better than to trust the count.

A pale gloved hand beckons once from the black doorway ahead, patient and unhurried, certain you will come closer on your own. The Nightbringer never needs to hurry when its prey is already boxed between two dead ends. It only smiles wider the longer you hesitate, waiting for the exact moment you decide to run.

Sewer grates rattle loose underfoot where the tunnel branches beneath the old tenements, one way leading toward the docks and open air, the other toward a silence that presses on your ears like water. A cold wind rises from that second branch, carrying the smell of old blood and older, colder stone.

Something with too many teeth for any human smile steps from the dark fork ahead, gloved hands spread in mock welcome. Its cloak drags through standing water without a ripple, as if the tunnel itself refuses to acknowledge it. The Nightbringer speaks softly here, courteous as ever, and the words land colder than the water at your ankles.

Rooftop tiles clatter loose where the chimneys crowd close together above the fork in the street below, and you climb because the ground route is already lost to fog too thick to see through, cold and rising fast. A shape crouches against that fog at the roofline, cloak pooling like spilled ink around gloved, folded hands.

It tips its head slowly, listening for your breath the way a cat listens for a mouse behind a wall. The Nightbringer has all the patience the long night can offer, unhurried, certain, smiling faintly at the sound of your heartbeat. You do not have that luxury, and the clock behind your eyes already knows it.

The bell tower creaks above a crooked branch of alleyway where the cobbles turn slick with old standing rain, and somewhere close a courier's dropped satchel lies split open, papers scattering white against the dark stone. You crouch to gather what you can, and that is when the fog beyond it moves on its own.

A tall figure steps free of it, smiling like it already knows exactly how this ends, gloved hand extended in an offer of help that is no offer at all. The Nightbringer's courtesy is a blade worn smooth from long use, hidden until the last possible moment. Prove tonight, at least, that its confidence is misplaced.

Lamplight gutters weakly along the narrow fork where the guild district splits from the slums below, one branch bright with candlelit windows, the other black as a shut and rusted door. Something moves in that darker half, unhurried, patient, gloved fingers trailing slow along the damp brick as it walks.

The Nightbringer only ever chooses the darker branch of any road, and tonight, so did you, though you told yourself it was the shorter way home. Its smile widens the moment it realizes you understand the trap now, far too late to matter. Type quickly, because understanding alone will not save you here.

Mist condensing thick on the cobbles turns the whole crooked street into a dim and trembling mirror of lamplight, doubling every shadow twice over. Where the road splits near the old well, a pale shape waits with its back against a shuttered window, cloak hanging unnaturally still despite the wind pulling at every loose shutter nearby.

Its eyes open slow, catching the last dying streetlamp like caught flame, and for a moment the whole alley seems to hold its breath along with you. The Nightbringer studies you the way a jeweler studies a stone, weighing worth before the first strike. Type before it blinks again and decides you are worth taking.

A dropped coin rolls to a stop at the fork in the alley, spinning once on its edge before settling heads up in a puddle of old rain that has not yet stopped falling. Fog swallows the sound of the second footstep that was never yours to begin with, close behind and softer than it should be for something that size.

A tall shape leans out slow from behind a rain barrel, smile widening like a wound finally allowed to open. Gloved fingers curl once in invitation, courteous as ever, patient as the fog itself. The Nightbringer collects far more than coin on nights like this, and it has already decided what it wants from you.

The tenement stairs branch into two crooked landings partway up, one lit by a single guttering candle in a cracked window, the other pitch dark and utterly silent despite the storm still hammering the shutters outside. Something in that dark landing exhales slowly, cold enough that your breath fogs the nearest broken pane.

A gloved hand rests on the broken railing there, patient, unhurried, waiting for you to decide which landing is the lesser risk. The Nightbringer already knows which one you will choose, because it always does. Climb fast, or let it climb first and meet you at the top instead.

Rain drums hard on tin roofs where the alley forks past a boarded and abandoned tavern, gutters roaring with runoff toward the black mouth of a sewer grate below. A tall shape steps out into the downpour entirely unbothered, cloak dry despite the storm soaking everything else in sight.

Its smile is far too handsome for the teeth waiting behind it, courteous even as it studies you head to boot like a merchant weighing goods. The Nightbringer offers shelter from the rain in a voice smooth as poured wax, patient and warm and utterly false. Type quickly, because its shelter always costs more than the weather ever would.

The lamplighter's ladder still leans exactly where he left it, abandoned at the fork where the crooked street splits toward the fog-choked docks. Fog curls around its rungs like curious fingers testing the wood. A voice, quiet and unnervingly courteous, offers a greeting from the dark just beyond the light.

The Nightbringer always sounds kindest right before it strikes, its words smooth and patient, its smile hidden just past where the lamp still reaches. You grip your line tighter and refuse to answer its greeting with anything but speed. The fog waits, patient as its master, for you to falter first.

Cobblestones glisten wet at the branch where three narrow alleys meet under one cracked streetlamp, its light flickering weak and gold across the puddled stone. A shape unfolds slowly from the deepest pool of shadow, taller than the doorway it stands beside, patient as the fog curling at its boots.

Gloved fingers curl once in silent invitation, and its smile widens the moment you square your shoulders instead of running. The Nightbringer respects nothing so much as prey that chooses to stand. Answer with your own steady hands now, and win this fork before the lamp above finally dies for good.

A church bell tolls once as the road forks past the crumbling chapel wall, and the sound dies fast, swallowed whole by fog thick enough to hide a second footstep behind your own. Something pale crouches low on the chapel's broken wall, cloak pooling around its boots like spilled ink left to dry.

It rises slow, joints unhurried, smiling with a courtesy that chills far more than the wind ever could on its own. The Nightbringer has waited here through worse storms than this one, patient as the stone beneath it. Fight now, before its patience finally runs its course and the courtesy drops away.

The gutter runs black with old standing rain where the alley splits behind the tanner's shuttered shop, carrying a single lost ribbon downstream toward the drain. A tall shape steps from the fork's darker branch, smile handsome, eyes hollow and patient as a held breath waiting to be released.

It bows low, mocking, gloved hand extended in an offer that is really no offer at all. The Nightbringer always offers the first move to prey it considers worth the courtesy, and tonight, whether you wanted it or not, that prey is you. Take the offer as a warning instead of a kindness, and answer its bow with speed of your own.

Fog rolls thick through the fork where the cobbler's row meets a dead end lit by one dying, guttering lamp, and the smell of old leather lingers even here where nothing living remains open. A shape detaches from the brick wall itself, cloak dragging soundless over wet stone toward the light.

Its smile is quiet, its patience quieter still, the kind of stillness that has had centuries to perfect itself. Type your line before that patience finally runs out and the quiet breaks into something far worse. The Nightbringer has never once needed to raise its voice to be terrifying, and it does not start tonight.

The crooked lane splits at an old covered well, one branch leading toward the guild hall's warm windows, the other toward a silence that presses close and cold. Mist pools thick around the well's cracked stones, rising slow like breath from something buried far beneath.

A tall figure rises from beside the well, gloved hands folded politely, waiting with the patience of something that has never once needed to hurry. The Nightbringer offers you first choice of branch tonight, courteous as ever, though both branches lead back to the very same waiting smile in the end. Choose speed over hesitation, always.

Loose shutters bang somewhere above the fork in the alley, rattled again and again by a wind that smells faintly of copper and old rain. A pale shape steps slow from behind a rusted rain barrel, cloak whispering against damp brick, smile widening at the exact pace of your own rising unease.

Its courtesy is a mask worn thin from long use, barely hiding the hunger underneath the handsome face. Strike before the mask finally slips entirely and shows what waits behind it. The Nightbringer prefers its prey believing in the mask a moment longer than they should, and you have already given it that moment.

The lamplight flickers twice and finally dies at the branch where the market street narrows down to a crooked throat of old stone. Something waits just past that dark, patient as a held breath, close enough now that you can hear the faint whisper of a cloak settling against the wall.

A gloved hand beckons once from deep in the shadow, unhurried, certain you will take one more step regardless of better judgment. The Nightbringer only ever needs that single extra step, no more, and it has learned exactly how to make prey offer it freely, without ever once raising a hand of its own to force the matter.

Rain pools deep in the cracked cobbles where the road forks near the old bell tower, reflecting a streetlamp that gutters weak and gold across the wet stone below, unbroken until now. A tall shape steps directly into that trembling reflection, cloak dragging soundless, as if the puddle itself refuses to ripple beneath it.

Its smile is handsome enough to be believed by anyone who has not already learned better. Do not believe it, no matter how warm its voice sounds through the rain. The Nightbringer has drowned better judgment than yours in reflections just like this one, and it is patient enough to try again tonight.

A courier's cart sits abandoned at the fork in the road, one wheel shattered clean through, papers scattered pale across the wet cobbles like fallen leaves. Fog swallows the street beyond it whole, thick enough that the far lamp is only a smear of gold through the grey.

Something tall steps slow from that fog, gloved hands folded in mock welcome, smile courteous and colder than the night around it. The Nightbringer always finds the ones who linger too long over scattered papers and broken wheels, and it has found you exactly on schedule. Move fast, before the fog closes the gap for good.

The alley forks past a shuttered bakery, the smell of old bread long lost beneath damp stone and colder, sharper air rising from the sewer grate somewhere below. A pale shape crouches on the sill above the door, cloak pooling like ink around its boots, patient as a spider waiting out a fly.

It drops without a sound, landing soft between you and the only lit window left on the street, smile already waiting before its boots even touch the ground. Race the clock now, before it finishes the smile it has clearly been saving. The Nightbringer never wastes a good entrance on prey too slow to appreciate it.

Mist thickens where the crooked street splits near the old tannery, carrying the smell of old blood that no amount of rain has ever managed to wash clean from the stones. A tall figure steps from the fork's darker half, gloved hand extended in mock welcome, smile patient and courteous.

The Nightbringer bows low at the fork, smiling wide enough to show exactly what waits behind the handsome face. Answer its bow with speed instead of courtesy of your own, because every second spent bowing back is a second it spends closing the distance between you both, unhurried and certain of the outcome.

The gutter overflows at the fork behind the guild hall, carrying a dropped glove past your boots toward the black mouth of the drain below. A shape unfolds from the shadow beside the drainpipe, taller than it has any right to be, smile patient, eyes catching the last lamp like a struck match.

It watches you a long moment before moving, unhurried, certain of its own patience the way only something ancient can be. The Nightbringer has stood in worse fog than this waiting for lesser prey to make the first mistake, and it will happily wait a while longer. Fight now, and refuse to be the one who makes it tonight.

A dead streetlamp marks the branch where the alley splits toward the fog-choked docks, its glass long since broken by some other night's forgotten trouble. Fog pools thick and cold around your ankles, rising slow the way water rises in a filling tub, patient and unhurried.

Something pale steps from the dark half of the fork, gloved hands folded, smiling slow enough that you can watch the smile grow the whole way. The Nightbringer likes an audience for its patience, even when that audience is only the prey it intends to take. Type fast, and deny it the satisfaction of watching you hesitate.

The crooked lane narrows to a single branch behind the old chapel, walls pressing close on either side until there is barely room to raise a weapon properly. A tall shape waits there already, cloak dry despite the drizzle soaking everything else, smile courteous and colder than the stone around it.

It inclines its head once in greeting, patient as the chapel wall itself, before straightening to its full unnatural height. The Nightbringer never needs to chase prey that has already walked itself into a corner this narrow. Make the corner work for you instead, and answer its greeting with your fastest hands.

Rain hammers hard on the tin roofs above the fork where two alleys meet near a boarded and forgotten inn, gutters roaring toward the black sewer grate below like a river finally finding its bed. A shape steps out unbothered by the storm entirely, gloved hand rising in a quiet, courteous greeting.

The Nightbringer's smile is warmer than the weather around it, and that warmth alone should be warning enough not to trust a single word it offers. Do not trust it regardless, no matter how cold the rain runs down your collar. Type through the storm, and let speed answer where trust never should.

The lamplighter's route ends at this fork tonight, his ladder abandoned against a crooked wall streaked dark with old standing rain and older neglect. Fog curls thick around the dead lamp overhead, swallowing what little light remains of the evening. A tall figure steps slow from the dark just beyond it.

Its smile is patient, gloved hand extended in an offer wrapped in false courtesy. The Nightbringer answers hesitation with an invitation every single time, smooth and unhurried, certain the invitation will be enough on its own. Answer quickly instead, before the fog closes in around you both for good.

Fog rolls off the gutters in slow grey sheets, pooling around a crooked lamppost at the exact spot where the alley splits in two. One branch climbs toward the bell tower and its promise of open sky. The other slides down into shadow that seems, impossibly, to breathe on its own, and the streetlamp above it gutters weak and gold, refusing to fully die.

You choose your footing carefully, boots finding the dry stones between puddles, and that is when the shape steps free of the mist. It is taller than any doorway has a right to allow, gloved hands folded, smile far too handsome for what waits behind it. A cloak drags soundless across the wet cobbles, leaving no trail, no ripple, nothing but silence in its wake.

The Nightbringer has already chosen its branch tonight, patient as the fog itself, and it studies you the way a jeweler studies a stone before the first cut. Somewhere close a shutter bangs loose in the wind, again and again, like a second heartbeat keeping time with your own. Now it only needs you to choose wrong, to hesitate one beat too long at the fork.

Steady your grip. The lamplight will not hold much longer, and neither will the quiet. Type your line clean and fast, because the Nightbringer has all the patience the long night can spare, and you do not.

Rain slicks the rooftops above the first branch of the old quarter, where loose tiles slide underfoot and gutters spill silver rain down into the dark street below. You climb because the ground way is blocked, because a courier's overturned cart has spilled crates across the only door, and because the fog down there is thick enough to hide a second footstep behind your own.

Somewhere above, a shape crouches on a crooked chimney, watching with eyes like dying embers, waiting for you to look up before it moves at all. It drops without a sound, cloak snapping open like leather wings catching wind, and for one long heartbeat you see its whole shape against the fogged moon, elegant and wrong, before it lands soft as falling ash between you and the ladder home.

Its smile is patient, courteous even, gloved hands folded as if this were a proper introduction rather than an ambush timed to the second. The Nightbringer never wastes a good entrance on prey too slow to appreciate it, and it clearly expects you to appreciate this one fully before the fight begins in earnest.

Race the clock now, because the Nightbringer never falls twice from the same chimney, and the rain shows no sign of easing before this is settled one way or the other.

The lamplighter never reached this corner tonight. His ladder lies broken against the crooked wall, rungs snapped clean through, and the lamp above it gutters weak and dying, throwing long trembling shadows across the wet stone. In his place waits a tall shape in a fine coat, gloved hands folded with unsettling patience, the kind of stillness that only comes from something that has stopped needing breath entirely.

It bows low when it notices you watching, mocking and courteous all at once, before its smile splits far too wide for any handsome face to hold steady. Somewhere behind it the fog thickens, swallowing the last dim light of the guttering lamp, and the whole alley seems to lean in closer to listen.

The Nightbringer offers you the first move only because it already knows how the second one ends, and its confidence sits on it like a well tailored coat, unhurried, certain, patient as the crooked wall it leans against. A dropped glove lies forgotten near the broken ladder, the lamplighter's, and you wonder briefly where the man himself has gone.

There is no time left to wonder further. The fog is closing, the lamp is dying, and the Nightbringer's bow is already straightening back into something far more dangerous. Type now, before the courtesy runs out entirely and the real fight begins.

Fog thickens at the fork where three narrow streets meet beneath a dead streetlamp, its glass cracked from some earlier night's trouble long before you ever arrived. A door creaks somewhere close in the dark, though no hand ever opens it, and the sound repeats twice more before it finally stops, leaving only the drip of rain from a broken gutter overhead.

You steady your grip and step forward anyway, because the branch behind you is worse, choked with old rubble and a smell you would rather not identify. A pale shape detaches itself from between two shuttered market stalls, cloak dragging soundless through spilled rainwater, its boots never quite seeming to touch the ground beneath them.

Its eyes catch the last thread of lamplight like a struck coin, bright and cold and entirely too aware. The Nightbringer studies you a long moment, unhurried, before it finally decides you are worth the trouble of a proper fight rather than a quick and quiet end in the dark.

Somewhere close a shutter bangs once more, as if the street itself is applauding the decision. Grip your line and hold steady, because whatever patience the Nightbringer has shown so far is about to run out, and the fork behind you offers no better shelter than the one ahead.

A crooked signpost marks the branch in the road just past the tannery, one arm pointing toward the guild hall's warm lit windows, the other snapped clean off by some older storm and never repaired. Mist pools ankle deep here and swallows the sound of your own footsteps entirely, so that you cannot tell if you walk alone or if something keeps perfect pace just behind your shoulder.

Something tall unfolds itself from the black mouth of a doorway, cloak whispering against old brick like a held breath finally released after a long and patient wait. The Nightbringer has been waiting here a long while, patient as stone, gloved hand rising in a slow and terrible welcome that somehow still manages to look courteous.

You remember, too late, that the signpost's broken arm always seemed to point straight at this very doorway, as if someone long ago had tried to warn every traveler who passed and failed to make the warning stick. The mist thickens further, curling around your boots like fingers testing whether you intend to run.

Type your line before that patience finally runs its course, because the Nightbringer's smile has already stopped pretending to be anything but hunger, and the broken signpost offers no direction now that matters half as much as speed.

Gutters overflow along the tenement row where the alley splits into two uneven halves, carrying dead leaves and a single dropped glove past your boots toward the drain, spinning slow in the black water. Somewhere above, a shutter bangs loose in the wind, again and again, like a heartbeat that will not settle no matter how long you wait for it to still.

You press on toward the darker branch because it is the only one that leads home, past shuttered windows and a crooked chimney leaning further than any chimney should. A shape detaches from the fog there, taller than any door frame should allow, cloak pooling around gloved boots like spilled ink left to dry on old stone.

Its smile is handsome and wrong all at once, courteous in a way that makes your skin go cold despite the mild night air. The Nightbringer only ever waits this long for prey it considers worth the wait, and it studies you now with the patient attention of a collector eyeing a particularly fine addition.

The overflowing gutter roars on behind you, indifferent to everything unfolding at the fork. Grip your line, because whatever courtesy the Nightbringer offers next will not last, and the darker branch has already proven itself the wrong choice to have made alone.

The streetlamp gutters twice and dies at the exact spot where the alley forks, plunging the whole crooked lane into a dark thick enough to taste, heavy with old rain and colder stone. Somewhere close a cloak drags slow across wet cobbles, and you count the seconds between each footstep, though you know better than to trust the count when nothing about this street behaves as it should.

A pale gloved hand beckons once from the black doorway ahead, patient and unhurried, certain you will come closer on your own without needing to be asked twice. The Nightbringer never needs to hurry when its prey is already boxed between two dead ends, one choked with rubble, the other guarded by its own smiling patience.

It only smiles wider the longer you hesitate, waiting for the exact moment you decide to run, as if the running itself were half the entertainment it came here for tonight. Somewhere behind the dead lamp, water drips steady from a broken gutter, marking time neither of you seem willing to acknowledge openly.

Whatever choice you make now, make it fast. The Nightbringer's patience is a well it has never once run dry, but yours is considerably shallower, and the fork offers no third branch to escape down.

Sewer grates rattle loose underfoot where the tunnel branches beneath the old tenements, one way leading toward the docks and open air, the other toward a silence that presses on your ears like water pushing in from every side at once. A cold wind rises from that second branch, carrying the smell of old blood and older, colder stone that no amount of rain has ever managed to wash clean.

Something with too many teeth for any human smile steps from the dark fork ahead, gloved hands spread in mock welcome, cloak dragging through standing water without a single ripple, as if the tunnel itself refuses to acknowledge its passing. The Nightbringer speaks softly here, courteous as ever, and the words land colder than the water already lapping at your ankles.

You check the branch behind you once, quickly, and find it just as choked with shadow as the one ahead, offering no comfort and no easy retreat. The grates rattle again somewhere close, though nothing visible moves to explain the sound, and the silence that follows feels far too deliberate to be accidental.

The Nightbringer waits, patient as the tunnel itself, gloved hand still extended in its mockery of welcome. Answer speed with speed now, because down here the fog cannot follow to warn you, and the only clock left running is the one in your own chest.

Rooftop tiles clatter loose where the chimneys crowd close together above the fork in the street below, and you climb because the ground route is already lost to fog too thick to see through, cold and rising fast enough to swallow the whole lane in minutes. A shape crouches against that fog at the roofline, cloak pooling like spilled ink around gloved, folded hands that have clearly done this before.

It tips its head slowly, listening for your breath the way a cat listens for a mouse behind a wall, patient enough to wait out an entire winter if the hunt demanded it. The Nightbringer has all the patience the long night can offer, unhurried, certain, smiling faintly at the sound of your heartbeat carrying clear across the narrow gap between chimneys.

You do not have that luxury, and the clock behind your eyes already knows it, ticking louder with every tile that shifts loose beneath your boots. Below, the fog continues to thicken, swallowing the last visible lamp along the street, leaving the rooftop as the only lit ground left in the whole crooked quarter.

Steady yourself against the chimney's crumbling brick, and do not look down. The Nightbringer is close enough now to smell the rain on your coat, and it has stopped pretending patience is anything but a courtesy it can withdraw at will.

The bell tower creaks above a crooked branch of alleyway where the cobbles turn slick with old standing rain, and somewhere close a courier's dropped satchel lies split open, papers scattering white against the dark stone like fallen snow that refuses to melt. You crouch to gather what you can, and that is when the fog beyond it moves on its own, curling in a way no ordinary wind explains.

A tall figure steps free of it, smiling like it already knows exactly how this ends, gloved hand extended in an offer of help that is no offer at all, courteous in the exact way a trap is courteous right up until it closes. The Nightbringer's courtesy is a blade worn smooth from long use, hidden until the last possible moment when it finally matters.

The bell above tolls once, unprompted, as if the tower itself has grown weary of watching this particular scene repeat. You leave the scattered papers where they lie and grip your line instead, because whatever the courier once carried matters far less now than what stands between you and the way home.

Prove tonight, at least, that its confidence is misplaced. The fog has already closed the gap behind you, and the bell tower offers no shelter, only a witness to whatever happens next in its shadow.

Lamplight gutters weakly along the narrow fork where the guild district splits from the slums below, one branch bright with candlelit windows and low laughter drifting faint through shuttered glass, the other black as a shut and rusted door that has not opened in years. Something moves in that darker half, unhurried, patient, gloved fingers trailing slow along the damp brick as it walks.

The Nightbringer only ever chooses the darker branch of any road, and tonight, so did you, though you told yourself it was the shorter way home and not simple exhaustion making the choice for you. Its smile widens the moment it realizes you understand the trap now, far too late to matter, the candlelit windows already well out of reach behind you.

A shutter somewhere in the bright branch bangs once, distant and unconcerned, as if that whole half of the street has no idea what unfolds so close by in the dark. You grip your line tighter, aware now that understanding alone will not save you here, not against something this patient and this pleased with itself.

Type quickly. The Nightbringer has already closed half the distance while you were busy noticing the difference between the two branches, and it does not intend to let you notice much else before this ends.

Mist condensing thick on the cobbles turns the whole crooked street into a dim and trembling mirror of lamplight, doubling every shadow twice over until you can no longer tell which belongs to you. Where the road splits near the old well, a pale shape waits with its back against a shuttered window, cloak hanging unnaturally still despite the wind pulling at every loose shutter nearby.

Its eyes open slow, catching the last dying streetlamp like caught flame, and for a moment the whole alley seems to hold its breath along with you, the mist itself pausing mid curl. The Nightbringer studies you the way a jeweler studies a stone, weighing worth before the first strike, unhurried despite the storm clearly gathering overhead.

You glance once at the old well, its cracked stones slick with the same condensing mist, and find no comfort there either, only more reflected lamplight bending strange across wet rock. The shape at the window has not moved again, though its smile has widened by some small and deliberate degree.

Type before it blinks again and decides you are worth taking. The mirrored street offers no second path home, only the one fork, and the Nightbringer clearly intends to be the one who decides how you cross it.

A dropped coin rolls to a stop at the fork in the alley, spinning once on its edge before settling heads up in a puddle of old rain that has not yet stopped falling from a gutter somewhere above. Fog swallows the sound of the second footstep that was never yours to begin with, close behind and softer than it should be for something that size to manage so quietly.

A tall shape leans out slow from behind a rain barrel, smile widening like a wound finally allowed to open after being held shut too long. Gloved fingers curl once in invitation, courteous as ever, patient as the fog itself pooling thick around the barrel's rotted staves.

The Nightbringer collects far more than coin on nights like this, and it has already decided what it wants from you, weighing the decision with the same idle interest it might give any dropped trinket in the street. You leave the coin where it lies, because bending to retrieve it now would mean turning your back on the one thing in this alley that matters.

Grip your line instead, and hold it steady. Whatever game the coin was meant to start, the Nightbringer has already moved past it, closing the last of the distance while your attention was elsewhere.

The tenement stairs branch into two crooked landings partway up, one lit by a single guttering candle in a cracked window, the other pitch dark and utterly silent despite the storm still hammering the shutters outside. Something in that dark landing exhales slowly, cold enough that your breath fogs the nearest broken pane before you even reach the turn in the stairs.

A gloved hand rests on the broken railing there, patient, unhurried, waiting for you to decide which landing is the lesser risk between two choices that both feel equally wrong. The Nightbringer already knows which one you will choose, because it always does, and its patience carries the particular weight of something utterly certain of the outcome.

The candle in the lit landing gutters once, threatening to go out entirely, as if even the flame senses which way this is truly headed. You test the railing with one hand and find it colder than the storm outside should allow, colder than wood left out in rain has any right to be.

Climb fast, or let it climb first and meet you at the top instead. Either way the stairs end at the same place, and the Nightbringer has never once minded waiting at the top for prey to arrive on its own.

Rain drums hard on tin roofs where the alley forks past a boarded and abandoned tavern, gutters roaring with runoff toward the black mouth of a sewer grate below, loud enough to swallow most other sound in the street. A tall shape steps out into the downpour entirely unbothered, cloak dry despite the storm soaking everything else in sight, not a single drop clinging to the fabric.

Its smile is far too handsome for the teeth waiting behind it, courteous even as it studies you head to boot like a merchant weighing goods before naming a price neither of you will actually get to negotiate. The Nightbringer offers shelter from the rain in a voice smooth as poured wax, patient and warm and utterly false in every syllable.

You glance once at the tavern's boarded windows, hoping for some gap wide enough to slip through, and find none, only warped wood nailed tight years ago against exactly this kind of night. The rain shows no sign of slowing, and neither does the smile waiting patiently just a few steps closer than before.

Type quickly, because its shelter always costs more than the weather ever would, and the tavern behind you offers no door that still opens for the living.

A church bell tolls once as the road forks past the crumbling chapel wall, and the sound dies fast, swallowed whole by fog thick enough to hide a second footstep behind your own without any real effort. Something pale crouches low on the chapel's broken wall, cloak pooling around its boots like spilled ink left to dry undisturbed on ancient, crumbling stone.

It rises slow, joints unhurried, smiling with a courtesy that chills far more than the wind ever could on its own, the kind of smile that has clearly practiced patience for longer than the chapel itself has stood. The Nightbringer has waited here through worse storms than this one, patient as the stone beneath it, untroubled by rain or wind or the tolling bell above.

You glance once at the chapel's broken door, half hoping for some sanctuary the old stone might still offer, and find only more darkness waiting past the threshold, no different from the fog already pressing close on every side. The bell does not toll again, as if it too has decided this is not its business to interrupt further.

Fight now, before its patience finally runs its course and the courtesy drops away entirely, because whatever waited quietly on that broken wall has clearly finished waiting for tonight.

The gutter runs black with old standing rain where the alley splits behind the tanner's shuttered shop, carrying a single lost ribbon downstream toward the drain, spinning slow in water that smells faintly of old leather and older rot. A tall shape steps from the fork's darker branch, smile handsome, eyes hollow and patient as a held breath waiting to be released after a very long wait.

It bows low, mocking, gloved hand extended in an offer that is really no offer at all, the gesture so practiced it might have been rehearsed across a hundred identical forks on a hundred identical nights. The Nightbringer always offers the first move to prey it considers worth the courtesy, and tonight, whether you wanted it or not, that prey is you standing at this exact fork.

The ribbon catches briefly on a loose stone before the current frees it again, carrying it on toward the drain and whatever lies beneath the street. You do not look away from the bow long enough to watch it disappear, because looking away now would be exactly the mistake this courtesy is designed to invite.

Take the offer as a warning instead of a kindness, and answer its bow with speed of your own, because the tanner's shop offers no door that opens from this side of the fork tonight.

Fog rolls thick through the fork where the cobbler's row meets a dead end lit by one dying, guttering lamp, and the smell of old leather lingers even here where nothing living remains open past dark, the shuttered shopfronts long since gone quiet for the night. A shape detaches from the brick wall itself, cloak dragging soundless over wet stone toward the light, as if drawn there by the last warmth it offers.

Its smile is quiet, its patience quieter still, the kind of stillness that has had centuries to perfect itself into something almost peaceful, right up until the moment it isn't. The Nightbringer has never once needed to raise its voice to be terrifying, and it does not start tonight, letting the silence between you do most of the work instead.

You check the dead end behind the lamp once more, uselessly, already knowing there is no other way through this fork, no side alley, no back door left unlocked by some careless cobbler. The lamp gutters lower still, as if agreeing with your assessment of the situation.

Type your line before that patience finally runs out and the quiet breaks into something far worse than silence. The cobbler's row will still be standing come morning; whether you are standing with it depends entirely on the next few seconds.

The crooked lane splits at an old covered well, one branch leading toward the guild hall's warm windows and the murmur of a late crowd still awake inside, the other toward a silence that presses close and cold like a hand over the mouth. Mist pools thick around the well's cracked stones, rising slow like breath from something buried far beneath that has only just begun to stir.

A tall figure rises from beside the well, gloved hands folded politely, waiting with the patience of something that has never once needed to hurry toward anything it truly wanted. The Nightbringer offers you first choice of branch tonight, courteous as ever, though both branches lead back to the very same waiting smile in the end, a fact it seems entirely content to let you discover on your own.

You lean once toward the well, half convinced the stirring beneath it deserves attention too, before deciding the shape standing beside it is by far the more immediate concern. The guild hall's distant warmth flickers faint through fogged glass, tempting and entirely too far to reach without passing this fork first.

Choose speed over hesitation, always, because the Nightbringer has already made its choice clear, and the well's mist shows no sign of settling before this is decided one way or the other.

The lamplight flickers twice and finally dies at the branch where the market street narrows down to a crooked throat of old stone, the abandoned stalls to either side offering nothing but empty crates and torn awnings flapping loose. Something waits just past that dark, patient as a held breath, close enough now that you can hear the faint whisper of a cloak settling against the wall beside you.

A gloved hand beckons once from deep in the shadow, unhurried, certain you will take one more step regardless of better judgment, the same certainty a well set trap carries long before the prey ever notices the wire. The Nightbringer only ever needs that single extra step, no more, and it has learned exactly how to make prey offer it freely, without ever once raising a hand of its own to force the matter.

You test the narrowed street with one careful boot, and the stone underfoot feels no different from any other night, ordinary and cold and entirely unhelpful in warning you of anything at all. The torn awnings flap once more in a wind you cannot otherwise feel, as if the street itself is holding its breath alongside you both.

Refuse the step it wants. The market stalls offer no better shelter than the throat of stone ahead, but at least here you choose the ground yourself, rather than letting the Nightbringer's patient hand choose it for you.

The gutter overflows at the fork behind the guild hall, carrying a dropped glove past your boots toward the black mouth of the drain below, spinning once before it vanishes into the dark water rushing beneath the street. A shape unfolds from the shadow beside the drainpipe, taller than it has any right to be, smile patient, eyes catching the last lamp like a struck match flaring bright before it gutters.

It watches you a long moment before moving, unhurried, certain of its own patience the way only something ancient can be, entirely untroubled by the overflowing gutter soaking its boots without so much as a ripple disturbing its cloak. The Nightbringer has stood in worse fog than this waiting for lesser prey to make the first mistake, and it will happily wait a while longer if tonight requires it.

The guild hall's back wall looms close behind you, its windows dark at this late hour, offering no help and no door left unlocked for a stranger fleeing down this particular fork. The glove disappears fully into the drain with a small, final splash, swallowed the way the whole alley seems intent on swallowing everything else tonight.

Fight now, and refuse to be the one who makes it tonight. The Nightbringer's patience is a well without a bottom, but the fork behind the guild hall offers no second chance once its smile finally decides to move.

The Clay Golem stands at the mouth of Waypoint alley, shoulders wide as a doorway, fists like millstones, clay cracking faintly with every breath it does not take. Fog drips off the guild lamps overhead. You raise your blade and roll initiative, feet braced on wet cobbles, ready.

Rain has packed the golem's clay shin with grit, and every step it takes grinds like a cart wheel dragged over gravel, slow and certain. You watch the crack running up its chest, wondering if that is a flaw or old scarring, while fifteen feet of fog still separate you from the answer.

Lamplight catches the golem's shoulder as it turns, revealing a shelf of dried mud where a shield should be, cracked at the edges like old pottery. Somewhere above, a rooftop guild bell rings the hour. You plant your feet, breath frosting, and wait for the first swing to commit.

The alley narrows to a single crooked lane, gutters running black with rain and old ash. Clay fists the size of barrels crack the air as the golem swings, missing you by inches and splintering a hanging sign. You roll clear and come up already counting seconds until it turns again.

Somebody carved a symbol into the golem's back centuries ago, and it still glows faint copper under all the grime and river mud. You catch a glimpse of it as the creature spins, dust sifting from every joint, slow and heavy, and you know exactly where you mean to strike next.

Fog rolls thick down Waypoint's crooked street, and the golem emerges from it like a chimney come loose from some forgotten roof. Its footprints fill instantly with rainwater, dark little pools. You grip your weapon tighter, feet cold on stone, waiting for that grinding step to close.

The golem's fist connects with a shuttered door, and the whole frame folds inward with a crack like breaking ice on a frozen river. Dust and splinters drift past the lamplight in slow spirals. You use the noise as cover, sliding along the wall toward its flank while it hauls free.

Waypoint's sewers breathe cold air up through an iron grate, and the golem's shin catches on the bars as it turns, grinding loud. You see the crack spreading wider up its leg, feel your pulse in your throat, and press the advantage before the mud has time to knit itself whole again.

A guild lantern gutters out just as the golem raises both fists overhead, clay groaning under its own weight. You count the beat before impact, roll sideways across slick cobbles, and feel the shockwave through your boots as clay meets stone hard enough to crack the street open.

The golem does not chase so much as arrive, closing distance with slow, terrible certainty, mud sloughing from its arms. Rain slicks its shoulders to a dull shine. You circle wide, waiting for the gap between its swings, aware one clean hit from those fists could end this fast.

Somewhere behind the fog, a bell tolls the hour, and the golem answers with a step that shakes loose mortar from a nearby wall. You watch its chest crack wider with every motion, a fault line spreading like frost across old glass, and you aim your next strike straight for the seam.

The alley reeks of river mud and old smoke, and the golem's footprints leave puddles that never quite drain away. You feint left, drawing a fist that crashes into a barrel instead of your ribs, splintering the wood, and use the half second of recovery to close in with everything.

Clay dust hangs in the lamplight like snow that forgot how to fall, drifting slow past crooked rooftops. The golem lumbers forward, indifferent to the cold, indifferent to you, until your blade finally finds the crack running up its side and it staggers, grinding, off balance.

You hear the golem before you see it, a grinding shuffle echoing off brick walls slick with fog and old rain. When it rounds the corner, fists already rising toward the lamplight, you plant your back foot and roll straight into the fight instead of backing away down the alley.

The crack in the golem's chest widens with a sound like a house settling in winter, deep and final. Rain drums on cobblestones as you circle it, watching gray mud flake loose in curls. One more good hit, you think, and the whole crooked wall of clay finally comes down for good.

A guild banner, soaked through and heavy with rain, slaps against the golem's shoulder as it turns, and for a moment you see straight through the crack to something pale and glowing beneath the clay. You do not wait around to ask what that light might actually be made of.

The golem's fist grazes a lamppost, and glass rains down in bright little pieces that catch the fog like scattered coins tossed from a purse. You use the noise, the sudden light, the half second of chaos, to slip past its guard and drive your weapon straight into the crack.

Waypoint's crooked towers lean in close overhead, blotting out what little moon there is tonight. The golem's eyes, twin points of dull ember, track your every step across the wet stone. You breathe out slow, roll your shoulders once, and close the last few feet between you both.

Somebody sealed the golem's chest with a copper plate long ago, back when the guild still kept records, and centuries of rain have worn it thin as parchment. You spot the seam glinting under lamplight and know exactly where your next strike needs to land to do real damage.

The golem drags one leg through standing water, clay sloughing off in gray ribbons that dissolve in the current. It is losing itself a piece at a time, and you intend to help that process along with the point of your blade before it manages to reach you first.

Every guild in this district has a story about the golem at the waypoint, and none of them end well for whoever swung first without a plan. You are about to add your own version tonight, fists up, breath fogging in the cold, feet already moving across the wet stone.

The crack across the golem's shoulder catches lamplight like a river at dusk, gold and shivering and strange. You watch it widen with every step the creature takes, mud flaking away in gray curls, and you press forward before it can seal itself shut again in the cold.

Rain turns the alley to a black mirror, and the golem's reflection arrives a half second before the creature itself, fists raised, shoulders grinding low. You roll the dice in your head before you roll them for real and step forward into range, breath held, blade ready.

The golem's footsteps shake dust from centuries-old shutters overhead, and somewhere a cat yowls and bolts for the rooftops. You do not have that option tonight. You square your stance, plant your feet on the wet cobbles, and wait for the first swing to give you an opening.

A crack in the cobblestones marks where the golem last put its fist down in anger, deep enough to catch a boot heel if you are not careful. You skirt around it, watching the creature reset its stance, and time your approach to match its slowest, heaviest point of recovery.

Fog swallows the tops of the guild towers, leaving only lamplight and the golem's hulking silhouette below on the wet stone. It swings wide, missing you by a hand's width, and you answer with a strike aimed straight at the crack already spreading across its ribs and chest.

The golem's clay shoulder catches on a low awning, tearing the canvas loose in one long strip. Rain pours through the gap onto its head, and mud runs like tears down a face that was never meant to have one, dripping onto the cobblestones below in slow gray streaks.

You count the golem's steps the way you would count a slow drumbeat, each one grinding closer through the fog and lamplight. When it finally swings, you are already moving, already inside its reach, already striking hard for the widening crack along its chest.

A stray dog barks from a doorway as the golem passes, then thinks better of it and vanishes down a side street into the fog. You wish you had that option too, but instead you grip your weapon tighter and step into the lamplight to meet the grinding thing head on, alone.

The golem pauses at the crossing of two crooked streets, clay dust sifting from its joints like flour spilled from a torn sack. You take the pause as an invitation, closing the distance fast on wet stone before it decides, slowly and heavily, to start moving again.

Somewhere under Waypoint, sewer water rushes loud enough to hear even over the golem's grinding steps through the fog. You use the sound to mask your approach, sliding along wet brick until you are close enough to strike before it even manages to turn and notice you.

The golem's fist splits a rain barrel clean in two, water gushing across the cobbles in a sudden flood that soaks your boots through. You wade through the cold spray anyway, and drive your blade toward the crack already spreading fast up its heavy, grinding arm.

You find the golem waiting at the waypoint the way old debts wait, patient and unhurried, standing in a puddle of its own dripping clay. Fog hangs thick between the crooked towers, and the guild lamps burn low and orange, throwing long shadows across wet cobblestones. Somewhere a shutter bangs in the wind.

You circle once, testing the ground for loose stone, and the golem turns with you, slow as a millstone finding its groove. Its chest already carries a crack, hairline thin, and you wonder how many fights it survived to earn that scar. When it finally swings, the fist passes close enough to fog your collar, and you answer with a strike aimed straight for the seam, praying the clay gives before your arm does.

The rain started an hour before you reached the waypoint, and it has not let up since, turning every gutter into a small black river. The golem stands beneath a broken guild sign, motionless until you step within range, and then it moves all at once, shoulders grinding, fists rising like a drawbridge.

You dodge the first swing wide, boots slipping on wet stone, and feel the wind of the second pass close enough to chill your neck. The crack along its ribs catches lamplight for just a moment, gold through gray mud, and you commit everything to that single narrow window before the light moves on and the opening closes for good.

Waypoint smells like river mud and old smoke, the kind that clings to your clothes long after you leave. The golem waits at the crossing of two crooked streets, dust sifting from its joints in slow gray curls, patient as a locked door. You have fought smaller things that hit harder, and bigger things that hit slower, but nothing quite like this.

When it finally moves, the whole street seems to shake with it, mortar rattling loose from nearby windows. You slide beneath the first heavy swing, feel the crack in its chest catch your eye again, wider now than it was a moment ago, and drive your blade toward it before the mud has any chance to close back over the gap.

Somebody sealed this golem's chest with a copper plate centuries ago, back when the guild still kept careful records of things like that, and the plate has worn thin as old parchment under all that rain. You spot the seam the moment lamplight catches it, a hairline glow beneath layers of packed clay and grit.

The golem swings twice before you find your opening, each fist heavy enough to crack cobblestone, each miss shaking dust loose from the crooked buildings around you. On the third pass you slip inside its reach entirely, blade angled straight for that copper seam, and feel the whole fight tip, for just a moment, in your favor.

Fog rolls down Waypoint's crooked lane thick enough to swallow lamplight whole, and the golem steps out of it like a chimney that decided to walk. Its footprints fill with rainwater almost before it lifts its next foot, small dark pools scattered behind it like a trail someone forgot to hide.

You circle wide, watching the crack in its shoulder catch what little light survives the fog, gold and shivering, and time your approach to its slowest recovery. When the fist finally comes, you are already moving past it, already inside its guard, already driving your weapon home before the golem even finishes its swing.

The alley narrows just past the old guild hall, gutters running black with rain and centuries of grime, and that is where the golem chooses to make its stand. Clay fists the size of barrels crack the air as it swings, missing you by inches and splintering a hanging sign into kindling.

You use the wreckage as cover, sliding along the wall toward its flank while it hauls its arm free from a shattered awning. The crack along its ribs has widened since you first saw it, a fault line spreading like frost across old glass, and you know this fight will not last much longer either way. One more good strike and the whole wall of clay comes down.

A guild lantern gutters out just as the golem raises both fists overhead, clay groaning under its own considerable weight. You count the beat before impact and roll sideways across slick cobbles, feeling the shockwave through your boots as clay meets stone hard enough to crack the street wide open beneath it.

Dust drifts down from the impact like slow snow, catching what lamplight remains. The golem turns, unbothered, already resetting its stance for another swing, but you have already spotted the crack running fresh up its forearm, pale beneath the grime. You press the advantage before the mud has time to knit itself whole again.

You hear the golem long before you see it, a grinding shuffle echoing off brick walls slick with fog and old rain, patient and unhurried the way only something without lungs can afford to be. When it finally rounds the corner into lamplight, fists already rising, you plant your back foot and roll straight into the fight instead of retreating down the alley.

The first exchange leaves your shoulder aching and the golem's chest freshly cracked, a pale line spreading through packed clay like a river finding a new course. Rain drums steady on the cobblestones as you circle, waiting for the next opening, aware that one more clean hit from those fists could end this before you get the chance to land yours.

Somewhere under Waypoint, sewer water rushes loud enough to hear even over the golem's grinding footsteps, and you use the sound to mask your approach along wet brick. Guild lamps flicker overhead, throwing crooked shadows across the narrow lane where the creature finally turns to face you.

Its fist splits a nearby rain barrel clean in two, water gushing across the cobbles in a sudden flood that soaks your boots through the leather. You wade through the cold spray anyway, driving your blade toward the crack already spreading fast up its heavy grinding arm, and feel the clay finally give beneath the pressure of a well placed strike.

The golem pauses at the crossing of two crooked streets, dust sifting from its joints like flour spilled from a torn sack, and for a moment you almost believe it might just walk away. It does not. Instead it turns, slow and deliberate, fists already curling closed at its sides.

You close the distance fast while it resets, feet slipping on wet stone slick with fog, and land the first real hit of the fight square against the crack running up its chest. The impact shivers up your arm, but the golem barely staggers, mud flaking loose in gray curls that the rain immediately washes toward the gutter and the dark grate beyond.

Rain turns the alley into a black mirror, and the golem's reflection arrives a half second before the creature itself, fists raised, shoulders grinding low against the fog. You roll the dice in your head before you roll them for real, feet braced, breath held tight in your chest.

The first swing goes wide, cracking a lamppost instead of your ribs, and glass rains down in bright little pieces that scatter across the cobblestones like coins spilled from a torn purse. You use the chaos, the noise, the sudden light, to slip past its guard entirely and drive your weapon straight into the widening crack along its side.

The revenant's coat is buttoned wrong, one hole skipped from collar to hem, as though whoever dressed it that last cold morning had been in far more of a hurry than they ever wanted anyone else to notice at the time, and no one ever did, not once, not even now, twenty years and counting into whatever this has become.

You notice the small detail before anything else about it, before the chains, before the cold, before the hollow patient face turning slowly to track your approach down the alley. It is almost funny, in a bleak sort of way, that something so far past caring still wears its old mistakes so plainly. You do not let the almost-humor slow your hand. You strike, and the buttoned wrong coat finally, mercifully, comes undone.

Behind the guild hall the old well has been dry for decades, and yet something climbs out of it tonight anyway, dragging a chain that catches on the bucket rope still coiled at the rim from whoever used this well last. Cold rolls off it in a slow wave that fogs the stones around the wellhead completely.

You back away from the well's edge, giving yourself room to swing, and watch it haul itself fully upright with the unhurried patience of something that has nowhere left to be in any hurry. Its coat drips well water that should have run dry with everything else down there years ago. You meet its first lunge standing well clear of the wellhead, because falling in is not a mistake you intend to make twice.

The canal district after midnight belongs to fog and to whatever the fog decides to carry. You have walked this stretch of the causeway twice already tonight, boots echoing off boards that remember every crossing anyone has ever made across them, and both times the far bank stayed stubbornly out of sight behind white. The third time you do not walk. You wait, weapon steady, and let the thing that has been pacing you finally close the distance on its own terms.

It comes up out of the steam near the old mooring post, coat dragging, chains trailing from sleeves that were never sewn to hold anything so heavy. Twenty years it has haunted this exact stretch of water, ever since the flood took the last real ferry downstream and left the post standing over nothing but ropes and rust. It does not remember the flood specifically. It remembers only the shape of waiting, the patience of something that outlived its own reasons for staying.

You do not give it the satisfaction of another slow crossing. You step forward first, weapon raised, and the fog around you both seems to hold its breath the way it always does right before the first honest hit lands somewhere it matters. The revenant answers in kind, chains rattling loose against the frozen boards, hollow face turning to track your approach with the terrible calm of something that has already died once and found the whole process entirely unremarkable the second time it happened.

Sixteen floors of a leaning tower rise above the street, and every window in it has been dark for years except the one at the very top, where a single candle burns steady behind glass nobody has cleaned since the fire that hollowed the place out. You have heard the stories about that candle from half the guild district, how it never gutters, how the wax never finishes melting, how something climbs those rotted stairs each night just to relight what should have gone out decades ago.

Tonight you climb those same stairs yourself, boots finding purchase on wood soft enough to worry you with every step. The dust on the ninth landing has not been disturbed in a decade, except for one clear drag mark leading toward the banister, the kind a coat makes when its wearer no longer bothers lifting its own hem. You find the revenant there waiting, exactly where the mark ends, hollow face already turned toward the stairwell as though it heard you from the first step.

It does not speak, and it does not lunge immediately either. It simply reaches for the banister the way it must have the night everything went wrong, fingers passing through wood that remembers the weight of a body it once held up. You do not let that memory slow your hand. You raise your weapon, plant your feet against the rotted boards, and meet it there on the landing where the candlelight barely reaches, letting the dice decide who finishes the climb.

The old canal breathes steam even on the coldest nights, warm water meeting frozen air in ribbons that hide the towpath completely from anyone foolish enough to walk it after dark. You are exactly that foolish tonight, tracking a hunt that has led you past three shuttered chandleries and one drowned mooring post already, following a trail of dragged chain marks that never quite touch the ground the way real chains would.

The steam thickens as you near the old drawbridge, and somewhere inside that white the revenant is already moving, patient as rot working slowly through old timber, tracking the sound of your boots with a certainty that has nothing left to prove and all the time in the world to prove it anyway. You count your breaths in the dark, three, two, one, the way you always do before stepping into ground you cannot fully see.

It meets you at the towpath's narrowest point, where the water runs closest to the boards and the fog is thickest of all, coat dripping steam, ribs showing pale where the buttons gape open across a chest that gave up breathing decades ago. For one long moment neither of you moves, the whole canal holding its breath around you. Then it lunges, chains dragging soundless across wet stone, and you meet the attack exactly where you stand, weapon first, because retreating into deeper fog was never really an option worth taking.

Behind the shuttered bakery on the corner, a chain rattles once, then twice, then falls utterly silent in a way that somehow carries louder than the sound before it. You already know what that silence means, having heard it three times now this month alone, always in this same stretch of the guild district where the fever took half the bakers and nobody ever quite finished mourning them properly.

You round the corner slow, weapon ready, and find the alley empty except for fog pooling thick around a drain grate that has not been cleared in years. The revenant does not announce itself the way lesser hauntings might. It simply arrives, the patience of twenty years standing frozen at some forgotten crossroads compressed into a single unhurried step that somehow covers half the alley's length at once.

Its coat is buttoned wrong from collar to hem, one hole skipped entirely, the mark of hands that were shaking too hard on some final morning to count properly. You do not have time to feel sorry for whatever it used to be before the fight demands your full attention. It studies you the way a bell studies an hour it has not rung in decades, patient, unbothered, certain that hesitation is the only real threat left in this narrow little corner of the city. You raise your weapon first, and refuse to give it that particular satisfaction tonight.

A jar sealed with a single tear of blue wax sits on a windowsill above the alley, closed forever against grief nobody living remembers collecting in the first place. The revenant favors this particular corner most nights, drifting toward the jar as though it still remembers crying once, long before the coat it wears now stopped needing warmth or company or anything else the living still ask each other for.

You have watched it pause here twice before without striking, curious more than cautious, wanting to understand what exactly draws it back to this one window out of every window in the whole fogbound street. Tonight you finally understand. The jar was its own, sealed by hands that are not attached to the coat anymore, grief bottled up and left behind the way everything else about it got left behind eventually.
