The moment breaks the way moments always break around it, sentiment giving way to the older, colder patience of something that has nothing left to lose by waiting one more year, or one more century. Chains it never needed drag across the cobblestones, and the fog thickens around your boots like it wants to hold you exactly where you are standing. You do not let the sadness of the jar slow your hand. The dice favor whoever strikes first and steadiest, and tonight that has to be you, whatever the jar once held.

Steam pours off the storm grates near the old mill until the whole street blurs into a single gray smear against a darker sky above, and somewhere inside that haze something moves with the unhurried certainty of a thing that has already died once and found the second time entirely unremarkable. You have tracked it here from three streets over, following chain drags that never quite touch the ground the way real iron would.

You back toward the mill wall, giving yourself something solid at your spine before the steam finally gives up whatever shape it has been hiding. The revenant emerges slow, coat dripping, ribs showing pale beneath buttons that gape open across a chest that stopped needing breath decades before either of you were born into this particular fight. It does not rush the reveal. It never rushes anything anymore.

You meet it there against the mill wall, weapon raised, letting that solid stone be the only retreat you allow yourself tonight. Chains rattle loose against the cobblestones as it closes the last of the distance, patient as ever, cold rolling off it in a wave that fogs the air between you completely. The fight that follows is fast despite all that patience, settled in the end by whoever refuses to hesitate first, and tonight you have already decided that hesitation is a luxury this particular street cannot afford you.

The old ferryman's post has stood empty at the mooring for twenty years, ropes frayed down to threads, and no boat has tied up there since the flood carried the last real ferry downstream along with half the district's memory of what the canal used to look like before the water rose. Twin lanterns still swing from posts nobody bothers to light anymore, creaking against a wind you cannot actually feel yourself.

Tonight something climbs out of the black water anyway, dragging behind it the shape of a coat it no longer needs to keep dry, water pooling black wherever its footsteps land on boards that have not held real weight in decades. You watch from the causeway, weapon ready, breath held tight against a cold that has nothing to do with the season or the hour or anything the calendar could actually explain to you.

It crosses the mooring slowly, the way something crosses when it already knows exactly how a crossing usually ends for whoever waits at the other side. Between the lantern light and its own shadow, the revenant seems to flicker in and out of being solid at all, as though even it has not fully decided which version of itself intends to finish this particular night. You do not wait for it to decide. You strike first, weapon steady, and hope the dice favor the quick over the patient for once.

Rain drums steady on tin roofs above the guild district tonight, loud enough to cover most sounds except the one you are specifically listening for, the slow scrape of something dragging itself up from a drowned cellar nobody has opened willingly in years. You wait at the top of the cellar stairs, lantern shuttered, letting your ears do the work your eyes cannot manage in this particular dark.

Clawed fingers find purchase on stone that remembers every single step ever taken across it, patient, cold, entirely indifferent to the storm rattling shutters three floors above where the guild still meets on nights when nobody remembers what waits below their own floorboards. The climbing takes longer than you expect, unhurried in the way only something with nowhere else to be can afford to be unhurried.

It reaches the doorway soaked completely through, coat heavy with cellar water, ribs showing pale where the buttons gape open across a chest that gave up breathing decades before the guild even built this particular hall. For one moment it simply stands in the frame, remembering doors, remembering thresholds, remembering every house it used to be welcome inside before the welcome ran out entirely. Then it steps through anyway, uninvited, and you meet it there because some fights only ever end at the doorway itself, storm or no storm.

The old golem frozen at the crossroads has worn rust for eyes since before anyone currently living in the guild district was born, standing sentinel over cobblestones slick with twenty years of fog nobody bothered clearing. Tonight something moves behind those rusted eyes, cold and patient, and you understand immediately that whatever rides inside that stone shape did not choose it by accident.

You keep your distance at first, circling wide, trying to read which way the whole assembly means to lunge once it finally commits to moving. It does not lunge so much as unfold, joints grinding loose after two decades locked in the same position, chains dragging from somewhere beneath the rust as though they grew there naturally instead of being carried in by whatever haunts the stone from the inside out.

The revenant riding it, or wearing it, or simply borrowing its shape for one convenient night, turns a hollow face toward you at last and waits, patient as the stone itself, for you to make the first careless mistake of the whole encounter. You do not oblige it. You close the distance yourself, weapon first, and let the golem's rusted joints discover exactly how much unremembered pain a body can still register even after twenty years of standing perfectly, patiently still.

Fog curls up thick from the storm drains along this stretch of the canal district, hiding your own boots from sight and turning the whole alley into a corridor of white you can only navigate by sound and stubbornness. Somewhere inside that white the revenant has been counting your footsteps for the better part of an hour now, patient, cold, closer than the sound alone would ever suggest to anyone unfamiliar with how it hunts.

You have learned, over three prior encounters in three different alleys, that stopping is the only move it does not expect from you. Running only ever fed the thing that has haunted these narrow streets for two decades running, and walking simply gives it more time to close whatever distance remains between its patience and your nerve.

So you stop, forcing it to close the last of that distance entirely on its own, and the fog finally parts enough to show a coat dragging across cobblestones, ribs showing pale beneath buttons gaping open in a way that has stopped surprising you by now. It does not hesitate once it sees you have stopped running. Chains rattle, cold rolls off it in a slow deliberate wave, and you raise your weapon and meet whatever comes next standing exactly where you chose to make your stand, fog or no fog, patience or no patience.

The gutters have run black for three nights running now, thick with old rain that never properly drained through pipes nobody has cleared since the flood, and somewhere beneath the cobbles something clawed has been dragging itself upward one floor at a time, patient as a bell that has not rung in twenty long years of standing silent in its tower.

You hear it long before you see it tonight, boots on rooftops overhead working steadily closer to whichever window it finally means to climb through first, and you have chosen your position accordingly, standing watch beneath the one window on this whole street that still holds a lit candle behind cracked and unwashed glass.

It surfaces at last through a broken grate three doors down, coat soaked black with runoff, hollow face tilting up toward that single lit window as though it remembers exactly which house used to be its own before everything about ownership stopped mattering to it entirely. You step between it and the window before it gets the chance to decide anything at all. The fight that follows is short, cold, and settled entirely by whoever lands the first honest hit, which tonight, finally, turns out to be you standing your ground.

Twin lanterns swing from a mooring post nobody has tied a boat to in twenty years, creaking against wind that never seems to actually blow through this particular stretch of the canal district, and between those lanterns the revenant drifts most nights, feet not quite touching the frozen boards it has haunted for longer than most of the guild has been alive to remember.

You have watched it circle that empty mooring three times now from a safe distance, always searching, always patient, as if some boat it once expected might still eventually arrive despite every reasonable indication that no boat is coming tonight or any other night for that matter. Tonight you decide to stop watching and start closing the distance yourself.

On your approach it notices you at last, and the searching stops all at once, replaced by something colder and far more focused than the aimless circling you observed before. Chains it never needed drag across the frost coating the mooring boards, and its coat, buttoned wrong from collar to hem exactly as it always has been, snaps once in wind you still cannot feel yourself. You grip your weapon, plant your stance, and refuse to let its cold reach any further than the very tip of your blade allows it to reach tonight.

Halfway across the causeway the fog thickens all at once, swallowing the far railing completely and turning what should be a simple crossing into something closer to a corridor with no visible end. Something pale steps out of that whiteness wearing a coat twenty years out of fashion, ribs showing through where the old buttons gape open wide across a chest gone hollow long before you were ever assigned to hunt it.

It says nothing at all, the way it never says anything, patient for you to make the first careless mistake of what should have been an ordinary crossing. You have crossed this causeway four times already tonight without incident, and you suspect that is exactly why it finally chose to appear on the fifth, having learned your rhythm well enough to know precisely when your guard would start to slip.

You do not oblige it the mistake it is waiting for. You hold your ground at the causeway's midpoint, weapon steady, and let the silence stretch until it decides on its own to close the remaining distance instead of waiting for you to close it first. When it finally moves, it moves all at once, chains dragging soundless across the frozen boards, and the whole fight condenses into three fast exchanges before the fog swallows whichever side loses this particular crossing completely.

Somewhere in the guild district a bell tolls the wrong hour from a tower nobody has climbed in years, its rope long since rotted through and its mechanism frozen by rust nobody bothered repairing after the fire. The revenant answers that wrong hour by drifting closer down the lane, unfinished business trailing behind it like smoke off a snuffed candle wick nobody ever relit properly.

You do not run from the sound of it, having learned the hard way three encounters ago that running only ever fed the thing that has hunted these narrow alleys for two decades running now, growing patient rather than reckless with every failed pursuit it has ever attempted against faster prey than you intend to be tonight.

It closes the distance at its own unhurried pace, coat dragging, chains dragging, every single part of it built for patience instead of speed the way most things that have already died once eventually become. When it finally reaches striking range the bell tolls again, wrong hour still, and you use that half second of its attention drifting toward the sound to land the first honest hit of the whole encounter. The fight that follows never quite escapes the bell's strange broken rhythm, each exchange landing slightly out of step with where you expect it to fall.

The longsword rusting in the fountain at the plaza's center has sat there since the flood took the whole square under six feet of water, green with age now and half sunk in silt nobody has bothered clearing in all the years since the water finally receded back to where it belonged. Tonight the revenant reaches for that longsword anyway, fingers made of fog closing around a hilt they can no longer actually grip.

Its hand passes through the rusted metal twice before something close enough to frustration turns its hollow face toward you instead, as if you have suddenly become the easier target compared to a blade it cannot lift no matter how many decades it spends trying. You recognize the moment for what it is, an opening it did not mean to give you at all.

You do not give it time to decide you make a worthier substitute for the sword it just failed to claim. You strike first, weapon steady, closing the distance across the plaza's cracked stones while it is still caught somewhere between frustration and whatever passes for memory in something that stopped needing living hands decades ago. The fountain's still water finally reflects something other than fog and old rust, if only for the length of this one decisive fight.

A blind alley off the canal ends in a locked door, and that is exactly where the revenant has been herding you for the better part of the last three streets, cornering you between crooked brick walls with nothing behind you now but fog and the sound of its slow, deliberate, entirely unhurried approaching feet closing the last of the distance between you both.

You test the door once, quick, shoulder against old wood, and find it exactly as locked as you feared it would be given how carefully this whole chase seemed engineered from the very first turn. There is no way out now but through, and you turn to face that fact squarely as the fog thickens further at the alley's only mouth.

Chains drag across wet brick behind you, and the particular cold that precedes it settles into the dead end like water finding its own level in a basin too small to hold it properly. You plant your feet against the locked door at your back and meet the revenant on ground it clearly chose for you, because sometimes the only choice left in a fight like this one is simply how hard you intend to make them work for it.

The chandlery on the corner has sat empty since its owner vanished one foggy autumn years before you were ever assigned to this stretch of the guild district, candles still lined up unsold in the dusty window behind cracked glass nobody ever bothered to replace after the owner failed to come back and explain what happened to them.

The revenant pauses there most nights, hollow face tilted toward those unsold candles as though it remembers exactly what they were meant for once, long before the cold took over entirely and made warmth an irrelevant concept to whatever it has become since. You have watched this particular pause three times now, always from the same doorway across the street, always wondering what exactly it is trying to remember.

Tonight you catch it mid pause, weapon already drawn, and it turns from the window slow and deliberate, chains dragging across a threshold worn smooth by two decades of this same nightly ritual repeated without fail regardless of weather or season. It does not rush you even now, confronted directly for the first time. It simply closes the distance at its own unhurried pace, and you meet it there, right at the door it can apparently never quite bring itself to open on its own.

Beneath the old drawbridge the water runs black and slow, carrying driftwood rafts past a mooring nobody has used since the flood took the last real boat downstream along with half the causeway's original lanterns. Something rides that current toward the bank tonight, patient, cold, dragging chains that catch on nothing but old habit and older rust accumulated over two full decades of exactly this same crossing.

You have waited at this exact bank for three nights running, having tracked the pattern of its crossings closely enough to know roughly when the current tends to bring it home. Tonight the pattern holds, and you watch it climb onto the muddy bank soaked completely through, coat heavy, boots leaving no print despite the obvious weight it somehow still carries after all this time.

For a moment it simply stands there dripping, remembering currents, remembering crossings, remembering every single trip it made across this exact canal before the last one finally went wrong for good. Then it turns toward you, having noticed your presence at last, and the fight that follows is fast, cold, and entirely unforgiving of hesitation on either side of the muddy bank you have both chosen to make your stand upon tonight.

Fog pours down the chute between two crooked towers tonight, thick enough to swallow the lamplight at street level completely and turn the whole intersection into a corridor of white nobody sane would willingly enter after dark. Out of it steps the revenant, moving with the terrible calm of something that has already died once and found the whole process entirely unremarkable the second time it happened, back when these same towers still stood straight and true against a sky that has not looked the same since.

You watch it settle into the alley below from your position at the mouth of the chute, chains dragging soundless across cobblestones slick with decades of accumulated fog, coat buttoned wrong from collar to hem exactly as it always has been in every encounter tracked so far this month. There is no surprise left in this meeting, only the familiar cold and the familiar patience and the sound of a door closing somewhere in a house that burned down long before either of you arrived here.

You raise your weapon anyway, because familiar does not mean safe, whatever comfort the routine might suggest to someone less careful than you have learned to be. It meets your approach the way it meets everything now, patient, cold, certain of an outcome it has rehearsed more times than you care to count, and the two of you settle into the fight standing exactly where the fog first brought you together.

The revenant's coat has been buttoned wrong for as long as anyone has tracked it, one hole skipped from collar to hem, as though whoever dressed it on that last cold morning had been in far more of a hurry than they ever wanted anyone else to notice at the time, and no one ever did, not once, not even now, twenty years and counting into whatever this particular haunting has become over that span.

You notice the small detail before anything else about it every time you face it, before the chains, before the cold, before the hollow patient face turning slowly to track your approach down whichever alley it has chosen for tonight's encounter. It is almost funny, in a bleak sort of way, that something so far past caring about anything still wears its old mistakes so plainly stitched into the fabric it never bothered fixing.

You do not let the almost humor slow your hand this time, however tempting the small absurdity of it might be in the middle of an otherwise deadly serious fight. You strike, weapon finding the gap where the buttons never quite lined up correctly, and the coat finally, mercifully, comes undone along with whatever held the whole haunting together for two decades running through this fogbound stretch of the city.

Behind the guild hall the old well has been dry for decades, capped over by planks nobody thought carefully enough to secure, and yet something climbs out of it tonight anyway, dragging a chain that catches on the bucket rope still coiled uselessly at the rim from whoever drew water here last before the well finally gave up producing anything but echo and cold air.

Cold rolls off it in a slow wave that fogs the stones around the wellhead completely, thick enough that you lose sight of your own boots standing barely three steps back from the rim itself. You back further away still, giving yourself proper room to swing without risking a stumble into the very hole this thing just climbed out of.

It hauls itself fully upright at last with the unhurried patience of something that has nowhere left to be in any particular hurry, coat dripping well water that should have run dry with everything else down there years and years ago. You meet its first lunge standing well clear of the wellhead itself, weapon steady, because falling into that same dark hole is a mistake you have absolutely no intention of making twice in one single night.

The old canal district goes fully quiet after midnight, every shop shuttered and every window dark except for the one candle nobody ever remembers to blow out on the corner of the abandoned chandlery, burning steady behind glass that has not been cleaned since its owner disappeared one foggy autumn years before you ever walked this particular beat.

The revenant favors that exact corner most nights, drifting past the flame like it half remembers what warmth used to mean before the cold became permanent and total, a habit worn into its patrol the way water eventually wears a groove into stone given enough patient, repeated years to accomplish the task properly.

Tonight you are waiting there instead of simply walking past on your usual round, weapon drawn, watching the candlelight throw strange shadows across a coat buttoned wrong from collar to hem in the way you have come to expect from every encounter with this particular haunting. It notices you the moment the shadows shift unnaturally against the chandlery wall, and closes the distance without hesitation, chains dragging soundless over cobblestones slick from a rain that stopped falling an hour before you ever arrived to wait for it.

Fog rolls low along the gutter and something in a gentleman's coat rises off a crate lid. Its hands are turned backward at the wrist, palms out, and when it smiles you count too many teeth. Rooftops lean close overhead. You grip your weapon and step onto the wet cobbles.

The lamplight flickers twice, then dies, and a shape unfolds from the shadow of the archway. Tiger eyes, human mouth, a voice like honey poured over gravel. It offers you a bargain you already know is a lie. Somewhere a gutter drips. You do not answer; you draw steel instead.

Steam hisses up from a grate as you cross the alley, and the fog parts just enough to show two backward hands folded in polite greeting. The face beneath the hood is almost kind, almost human, almost yours. You keep both eyes on the claws and neither on the smile.

A guild banner sags overhead, soaked through, while paw prints the size of dinner plates track across the gravel toward a shuttered door. Something purrs behind it, low and pleased, like it has been expecting company. You test the door. It is not locked. That is the trap.

The rooftops here lean so close that the sky is just a seam of grey between them, and in that seam a tiger's silhouette prowls, easy and unhurried. It has all the time it wants. You do not. Below, the sewer grate rattles once, as if something answered from underneath.

Between one lamppost and the next the fog thickens into a shape with reversed hands and a courtier's bow. It speaks your name wrong on purpose, testing how you flinch. The cobbles are slick with old rain. You plant your feet and refuse to give it the reaction it wants.

A chestnut vendor's cart lies overturned at the mouth of the alley, nuts scattered like teeth across the stone. Whatever tipped it left claw marks deep in the wood and a smell of hot fur hanging in the fog. Somewhere close, something purrs its approval of the mess it made.

The guild doors are barred from outside, which means whatever locked them is already in. Through the keyhole comes a low chestnut-warm chuckle and the click of backward fingers tapping wood. You raise your weapon. The lock gives way before you even touch it.

Rain slicks the rooftop tiles as you climb, and something with a tiger's face and a gentleman's hands watches from the next chimney over, entirely unbothered by the height. It offers a slow, mocking bow. You do not bow back. You close the distance at a dead run instead.

The sewer entrance breathes out warm, wrong air, and painted on the brick above it is a crude sigil someone scratched in a hurry. Claw marks. A door left ajar on purpose. Somewhere below, a voice like honey calls up an invitation you have no intention of accepting.

Fog pools thick around the lamppost, and within it something folds long fingers backward, knuckle to wrist, in a gesture meant to unsettle you. It works. You breathe once, twice, and step through the fog anyway, because the alternative is standing still and losing.

A cracked mirror hangs crooked in an abandoned shopfront, and in it your reflection smiles a half second before you do. Behind you, nothing. In the glass, a tiger's shadow slides past your shoulder. You do not turn around. You watch the mirror and time your strike.

The gravel path behind the guildhall is scattered with coins, too neat to be an accident, each one placed like a lure toward the dark. At the end of the trail a shape waits with backward hands folded, patient as a debt collector counting interest. You step past every coin and go straight for the throat instead.

Somewhere above the rooftops a cat yowls once and cuts off mid cry, and the silence that follows is far worse than the sound. You climb the fire escape two rungs at a time, fog curling cold around your boots, and find a tiger's grin waiting patiently at the top, entirely too pleased that you bothered coming to look for it.

The alley narrows to a single crooked door marked with a symbol scratched fresh into old wood, the paint of it still faintly wet. Behind it something purrs, content, entirely certain of its welcome. You test the handle. It turns without resistance. That ease alone tells you everything you need to know before you even step through.

A decanter of dark liquor sits untouched on an overturned barrel, offered up like a peace treaty nobody signed. Whatever left it there is still close, watching patiently from the fog with eyes that catch the lamplight wrong. You leave the decanter exactly where it sits and raise your weapon instead of a toast.

The rooftop gutter runs red with old rust or maybe worse, and the tiles ahead are scored with four parallel claw marks, deep enough to catch a careless boot heel. You follow them anyway, because turning back now means admitting the fog has already won this whole round of the chase.

A gentleman's hat rolls past your feet, empty, its owner nowhere in sight, and behind it comes a low chuckle from somewhere deep in the wet dark. You do not bother chasing the hat. You watch the alley mouth instead, where a shape with backward hands is already stepping calmly out to meet you.

The guildhall lantern gutters and relights itself, far too deliberate to be wind. Something out there is toying with the light the way a cat toys with a moth before it finally eats it. You keep walking anyway, boots loud on the wet stone, refusing to let the flickering dark decide how fast you move tonight.

A trail of chestnut shells leads from the market stalls into the fog, each one cracked open in turn like a slow countdown someone is running. At the last shell a tiger's tail flicks once from behind a chimney stack, and then the whole rooftop goes perfectly, deliberately still, waiting patiently for you to arrive.

The sewer grate below your boots hums faintly, warm, alive in a way plain iron should never be. A voice drifts up through it, honey smooth, offering safe passage for a price you never agreed to pay. You step off the grate anyway and find the stairs down on your own terms, not its.

Fog thickens at the mouth of a dead end alley, and within it a shape bows low with a courtier's grace, backward hands spread wide in welcome. The bow is theatre, all performance and no substance. The claws underneath are not. You answer the welcome with the flat of your blade, not a handshake.

A cracked shop window shows a tiger's reflection standing just behind where you are, though the street at your back is entirely empty. You do not look back to check, no matter how badly you want to. You watch the glass, count your own heartbeat, and strike the instant the reflection moves first.

The rooftops drip steadily with old rain and something paces the ledge above you, unbothered by the narrow stone, tail flicking with lazy patience. It has cornered prey here plenty of times before. You refuse to be the next one it does not even bother learning the shape of before it strikes.

A guild seal lies snapped clean in half on the cobbles, its wax still warm, whoever broke it barely gone from sight. The fog around the alley mouth smells thick of hot fur and old honey. Somewhere close a voice hums a tune that is almost a lullaby and entirely, unmistakably a threat.

The lamplight throws two shadows from one shape crouched on the gutter's edge, one human, one not, the two of them arguing silently over which face to wear tonight. You do not wait around for it to decide. You close the gap while it is still busy being two things at once, neither one ready.

A backward handprint presses wet into the fogged shopfront glass, five fingers splayed the wrong direction from any wrist you know. Whatever left it there is still close enough to watch you notice, still amused by your reaction. You back slowly toward open street and keep your weapon raised the whole way.

The alley behind the tannery reeks of curing hide and something else entirely, warmer, wilder, far more patient. Claw marks score the doorframe at exactly eye height, as if measuring you for a fitting you never asked for. You step past the marks and refuse to be measured for anything at all.

Rain needles steadily down through a broken skylight above a shuttered guildhall, and in the puddle below, a tiger's face ripples where your own reflection ought to be. You do not flinch, not even a little. You step straight through the puddle and let the fog take the reflection with it instead.

A rope ladder sways from a rooftop that should be entirely empty, and at the top a shape waits with a gentleman's posture and a predator's stillness, watching you climb with obvious, patient amusement. You climb anyway, rung after slow rung. You have never once, in your whole life, liked being watched.

The fog swallows the lamplight whole at the mouth of the sewer stair, and from somewhere down in that dark comes a low, satisfied purr, the sound of something that already believes its hunt is finished before it has even begun. You start down the stairs anyway, ready to prove it thoroughly wrong.

A guild apprentice's dropped satchel lies open on the cobbles, its contents entirely untouched, which is the single wrongest thing about the whole scene. Something out there wanted you to stop and look closer. You do not stop. You keep moving toward the alley where the real trail actually begins.

The fog over the tannery district never quite lifts, and tonight it carries a warmth that has no business coming off cold stone. You track the smell of hot fur down a gutter that runs uphill, past shopfronts shuttered so long the paint has peeled to bare wood. Somewhere a chestnut vendor's abandoned cart still smolders under a fresh dusting of ash.

At the end of the lane a gentleman in a very good coat waits with his hands folded behind his back, and when he turns to greet you, you see why. His fingers bend the wrong way at the knuckle, backward toward the wrist, and his smile shows one row of teeth too many. He offers a bow so perfect it could only be practiced. You do not return it. You draw your weapon, and somewhere close, something that is not quite a man begins, very softly, to purr.

Rooftops crowd so close over this quarter that daylight rarely reaches the cobbles, and by lamplight the gap between buildings looks like a single unbroken ceiling, fog pooling along its seams like smoke trapped indoors. You climb a rickety fire escape because the street below has gone strangely quiet.

At the top, a shape crouches on the ledge with a tiger's face and the poise of a courtier attending a garden party. It does not startle at your arrival; it seems to have been expecting you for some time, patient as rent coming due. Its tail flicks once, twice, gauging the width of the gap between your ledge and its own. You test your grip on the rail and refuse to look down, because down is exactly where it wants your attention, and up here you can still see its hands.

The sewer stair behind the guildhall breathes warm air up into the cold night, wrong in a way you feel before you understand it. You go down anyway, one hand on the slick brick, boots finding the worn centers of stairs other feet have used for a very long time. Water drips in a rhythm too even to be natural.

At the bottom a lantern burns behind a grate, and behind it a shape sits with knees drawn up and hands folded backward at the wrist, watching you arrive with the patience of something that has never had to chase its dinner. It speaks first, voice honey smooth, offering safe passage if you simply turn around. You do not answer. You raise your weapon, and the lantern gutters as if the thing behind the grate finally exhaled.

Fog rolls off the river and pools thick in the alley behind the fishmonger's stalls, carrying the smell of brine and something else underneath it, warm and wrong and close. A cracked mirror leans against a crate where someone left it to be collected, and in its glass your reflection stands a half second behind where you actually are, as if it is still catching up.

You do not trust the mirror, and you are right not to. A tiger's shadow slides across the glass behind your reflection, though the alley behind you shows nothing when you finally risk a glance. Somewhere close, a low chuckle rides the fog, delighted that you noticed. You turn your back on the mirror entirely and face the alley instead, weapon raised, because whatever this is, it wants you watching the wrong thing.

The guild seal on the door has been snapped clean in half, wax still faintly warm, and beyond the broken lock the hall sits dark except for one guttering candle someone left burning on purpose. Coins are scattered across the floor in a trail too neat to be an accident, each one placed like a lure toward the back room where the candle's light does not quite reach.

You follow the coins because the alternative is standing in the dark waiting for whatever scattered them to come find you first. At the end of the trail a shape rises from behind the guildmaster's overturned chair, backward hands folded, a tiger's face arranged into something almost like an apology. It is not sorry. You can tell by the way it is still smiling when you step past every last coin and refuse the invitation.

Rain slicks the rooftop tiles until every step is a gamble, and you take the gamble anyway because the alternative is the alley below, where something with a gentleman's coat and a predator's patience has been pacing for the better part of an hour. From up here you can see the whole crooked skyline, chimneys leaning together like old men gossiping in the wet dark.

A shape detaches from the shadow of the nearest chimney stack, unbothered by the rain, unbothered by the height, unbothered by anything at all as far as you can tell. Its tail flicks once in greeting, or maybe warning; with a tiger it is hard to say which. You plant your feet on the wet tile and refuse to give ground, because one wrong step here means a very long fall, and this thing knows it better than you do.

The alley behind the tannery reeks of curing hide and hot fur in equal measure, the two smells tangled so thoroughly you cannot tell where one ends and the other begins. Claw marks score the doorframe at exactly eye height, four parallel gouges deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, as if something paused here to measure you for a fitting.

You step past the marks anyway, because turning back means admitting the fog has already decided how this goes. Behind the door, low and pleased, something purrs at the sound of your approach, entirely certain of its welcome. When the door finally opens on its own, unlatched from the inside, a gentleman's silhouette stands framed in lamplight, hands folded backward at the wrist, offering you a bow you have no intention of returning.

A decanter of dark liquor sits untouched on an overturned barrel in the middle of the guild courtyard, offered up like a peace treaty nobody signed and nobody trusts. The fog around it pools strangely thick, curling close to the ground the way smoke does when there is no wind to carry it anywhere else.

Whatever left the decanter is still close, watching from just beyond the lamplight with eyes that catch the glow wrong, too bright, too golden, too patient. You leave the offering exactly where it sits and turn slowly to face the dark instead, weapon raised, because whatever wants you distracted by a drink is exactly the thing you need to be watching. A low chuckle answers your refusal, warm as honey and twice as false.

The lamplight along this stretch of the gutter flickers in a pattern too deliberate to be a dying wick, on and off, on and off, like something is testing how the shadows fall. You count the flickers without meaning to, and somewhere around the seventh one a shape steps fully into view where a moment ago there was only empty cobblestone.

It wears a gentleman's coat over a tiger's face, an arrangement that should look absurd and instead belongs perfectly to this rotten, dangerous street. Its hands are folded behind its back, bent backward at the wrist in a way that turns your stomach even from a distance. It offers a bargain in a voice like honey poured over gravel. You draw your weapon, and the lamplight steadies at last, as if it too was waiting to see what you would choose.

A trail of cracked chestnut shells leads away from the overturned market cart, each shell split open in turn like a countdown someone patient is running just for you. You follow it past shuttered stalls and a guild banner sagging heavy with rain, the fog thickening with every step until the lamplight behind you is only a smear of gold.

At the last shell, a tiger's tail flicks once from behind a chimney stack high above, and the whole rooftop goes still in a way that feels rehearsed, waiting for its cue. You look up just as the shape drops soundlessly to the cobbles ahead, landing in a crouch far too graceful for something so large. It rises into a gentleman's posture, brushing dust from its coat, and smiles with far too many teeth for the moment to feel like anything but a trap.

Water drips through a broken skylight above the shuttered guildhall, each drop landing in a puddle that has slowly grown wide enough to reflect the whole ruined ceiling. You cross the room carefully, boots silent on old floorboards, and try not to look down at the puddle more than you have to.

You look anyway, and the reflection staring back is wrong: a tiger's face where your own should be, patient and amused and entirely unbothered that you have noticed. You do not flinch, though every instinct screams to run. Instead you step straight through the puddle, scattering the reflection into a hundred rippling pieces, and somewhere behind you in the real dark, a low, satisfied purr confirms that the thing wearing your face was never really trapped in the water at all.

The rope ladder hanging from the rooftop should not be there, not on a building this abandoned, not swaying gently when there is no wind to move it. You test the first rung anyway, because the alley behind you has gone too quiet for comfort and the roof at least offers a clear line of sight.

At the top a shape waits with a gentleman's posture and a predator's patience, watching you climb with obvious, unhurried amusement, as though your effort is the evening's entertainment. It offers a hand to help you over the ledge, fingers bent backward at the wrist in a way no human hand should bend. You ignore the hand and haul yourself up on your own, weapon ready, because whatever help this thing is offering always, always comes with a price you have not agreed to pay.

The guild apprentice's satchel lies open on the wet cobbles, its contents perfectly undisturbed, which is the single wrongest detail in an alley full of wrong details. Coins, a stub of chalk, a half eaten chestnut, all arranged as if someone wanted you to stop and take inventory before continuing on.

You do not stop for long. Something wanted your attention here, and that alone is reason enough to keep moving toward the true trail, the one marked by claw gouges in the brick and a smell of hot fur growing steadily stronger. At the alley's end a gentleman's silhouette waits beneath a dead lamppost, hands folded backward, smiling the exact patient smile of someone who left the satchel there on purpose and is delighted that you noticed but did not linger.

Fog swallows the mouth of the sewer stair whole, thick enough that the lamplight behind you barely reaches past your own shoulder. From somewhere down in that dark comes a low, satisfied purr, unhurried and confident, the sound of something that already believes tonight's hunt is as good as finished before it has properly begun.

You start down anyway, one careful step at a time, letting your eyes adjust to the dark. At the bottom, a shape rises from a puddle, tiger faced, gentleman dressed, backward hands spread in a mocking bow of welcome. It compliments your nerve in a voice like honey over gravel, expecting the words to unsettle you more than the claws do. You raise your weapon instead of trembling, and for just a moment, its confident smile flickers.

The guild banner above the courtyard hangs sodden and heavy with rain, dripping steadily onto cobbles already slick from the storm that passed through an hour ago. Paw prints track across the gravel toward a shuttered side door, each print the size of a dinner plate and pressed deep enough to hold standing water.

You follow the prints because the alternative is waiting here in the open, and open ground favors whatever left tracks that large. Behind the shuttered door something purrs, low and pleased, entirely certain of its welcome, the sound of a predator that has never once had to work very hard for its supper. You test the door and find it unlocked, swinging open on oiled hinges that someone bothered to maintain, which tells you this welcome has been prepared for far longer than tonight.

Between one guttering lamppost and the next, the fog thickens into something with a shape, reversed hands folding into a courtier's bow so smooth it could only have been practiced for centuries. It speaks your name wrong on purpose, testing the shape of your flinch the way a tailor tests a hem.

The cobbles underfoot are slick with old rain and older grime, and you plant your feet carefully before answering with steel instead of words. It does not seem surprised by your silence; if anything, the tiger's mouth curls a little wider, as though silence was the answer it wanted all along. Somewhere behind it, a second lamppost gutters out on its own, plunging half the alley into a dark that feels suddenly, deliberately, much larger than it was a moment ago.

A cracked shop window along the tanners' row shows a reflection standing just behind where you are, though the street at your back is entirely, verifiably empty when your nerve finally breaks and you glance. You do not look back a second time. You watch the glass instead, counting your own heartbeat against whatever rhythm the reflection is keeping.

The tiger's face in the glass smiles a half second before your own reflection does, patient as a debt collector who already knows you cannot pay. You strike the moment the reflection's hand moves first, shattering the window in a spray of glass that sounds far too satisfying for how much your own hand is shaking. Behind you, in the real dark, something laughs low and warm, delighted that you finally decided to stop watching and start fighting.

The alley narrows until your shoulders nearly brush both walls, funneling you toward a single crooked door marked with a symbol scratched fresh into old wood, the paint of it still faintly wet to the touch. Behind the door something purrs, content and unhurried, utterly certain that whatever comes through will be worth the wait.

You test the handle and it turns without the slightest resistance, oiled and ready, which alone tells you everything you need to know before you ever step through. Inside, lamplight throws a gentleman's shadow twice as tall as the room should allow, backward hands already folded in greeting. It thanks you for coming so promptly, as though you had any real choice in the matter, and you answer by raising your weapon instead of returning the courtesy.

Steam hisses continuously from a broken grate as you cross the narrow alley, fog parting just long enough to show two hands folded in polite greeting, fingers bent backward at every knuckle in a way that makes the greeting feel like a threat dressed up in manners. The face beneath the hood is almost kind, almost human, almost, disturbingly, almost yours.

You keep both eyes fixed on the claws and neither on the smile, because the smile is clearly meant to be the distraction. It speaks in a voice like honey poured slow over gravel, offering terms you have no intention of considering. Somewhere behind it a second shape flickers into being and out again, a trick of fog and lamplight, or perhaps not a trick at all. You do not wait to find out which. You close the distance instead.

Rain hammers the tiles of a rooftop three stories above the guildhall, and you climb toward it because the street below has gone unnervingly still, the specific stillness that settles right before something large decides to move. Chimneys lean together against the wind like old friends sharing a secret they would rather not tell you.

At the top, a shape crouches easily on the narrow ledge, entirely unbothered by height or weather, a tiger's face turned toward you with an expression that might be curiosity or might simply be hunger wearing a patient mask. It rises slowly into a gentleman's posture, brushing rain from a coat that somehow stays perfectly dry. You grip the wet rail and refuse to look away first, because looking away, on a ledge like this, is the only mistake that actually matters.

The guild courtyard gate hangs open on one hinge, swinging slowly in a wind you cannot otherwise feel, and beyond it the cobbles are scattered with coins arranged in a line too deliberate to be anything but bait. Fog curls low around each coin, pooling thick enough to hide whatever waits at the trail's end.

You follow the coins step by careful step, counting them without meaning to, until the trail ends at an overturned chair and a shape rising slowly from behind it. Backward hands fold in greeting, a tiger's mouth curls into something almost like an apology it clearly does not feel. It calls the coins a gift, a welcome, an honest offer of hospitality. You step past every last one and answer with your weapon instead, because honest is the last word this thing has earned.

Fog pools thick around a lamppost at the alley's mouth, and within that fog something folds its long fingers backward, knuckle bending toward the wrist in a slow, deliberate gesture clearly meant to unsettle you before a single word is spoken. It works, at least a little; you feel your grip tighten on your weapon without quite deciding to.

You breathe once, twice, and step through the fog anyway, because the alternative is standing still while it decides how this goes, and standing still has never once won a fight in this city. The shape resolves fully as you approach, gentleman's coat over a tiger's face, hands already folding into a mocking little bow. It speaks first, voice warm as honey, but you are already moving before it finishes the sentence.

A guild lantern above the alley gutters and relights itself twice in a row, far too deliberate to be wind, the flame flickering in a rhythm that feels like a signal from something that wants very badly to be noticed. You keep walking anyway, boots loud and deliberate on the wet stone, refusing to let flickering light dictate your pace.

At the alley's end the lantern finally steadies, and in its restored glow a shape waits with a gentleman's posture, a tiger's face, and hands folded backward at the wrist in patient, practiced welcome. It compliments your composure, clearly disappointed that the flickering game did not rattle you more. You do not answer the compliment. You close the distance instead, weapon raised, while the lantern behind you burns steady at last.

The gravel path behind the guildhall glitters with scattered coins in the lamplight, each one placed with the precision of a lure rather than the carelessness of an accident, leading straight toward a shadowed archway where the fog pools thickest. Somewhere within that shadow, something waits with the patience of a debt collector counting interest.

You step past every coin without slowing, because stopping to collect them is exactly what the trail wants you to do. At the archway a shape steps forward, backward hands folded, tiger's face arranged into a courtier's polite smile. It calls the coins a small hospitality, a gesture of goodwill before the real business begins. You answer by drawing your weapon, and the polite smile widens into something with entirely too many teeth for goodwill.

The tannery alley reeks of curing hide, hot fur, and old rain in a combination that makes your stomach turn before you have even seen the source. Claw marks score the doorframe ahead at precisely eye height, four parallel gouges deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, as though something paused here once just to measure a previous visitor for a fitting.

You step past the marks anyway, refusing to be measured for anything tonight. Behind the door, low and pleased, something purrs at the sound of your footsteps, utterly certain of its welcome and entirely unbothered by your weapon. When the door swings open on its own, unlatched from within, a gentleman's silhouette stands waiting in lamplight, offering a bow you have absolutely no intention of returning in kind.

A cat yowls once above the rooftops and cuts off mid cry, and the silence that follows presses down heavier than the sound ever did, thick enough that you can hear your own pulse in your ears. You climb the fire escape two rungs at a time anyway, fog curling cold around your boots with every step upward.

At the top, a tiger's grin waits patiently in the lamplight, entirely too pleased that you bothered coming to look for whatever made that sound. It sits with a gentleman's posture, backward hands folded neatly in its lap, as though it has all the time in the world and knows you do not share that luxury. You raise your weapon and refuse to let the silence, or the grin, decide how this next moment unfolds.

The sewer entrance behind the fish market breathes out warm, wrong air that has no business rising from anything as cold as sewage, and painted crudely on the brick above it is a sigil someone scratched in a visible hurry, more warning than welcome. The door beside it hangs ajar, deliberately, invitingly.

Somewhere below, a voice like honey poured over gravel calls up an invitation you have absolutely no intention of accepting on its terms. You go down anyway, weapon first, boots finding the worn centers of steps that countless other feet have used before yours. At the bottom, a shape waits with a tiger's face and a gentleman's folded hands, delighted that you came, disappointed that you came armed and unwilling to talk.

Rain slicks the cobbles outside the guildhall until every surface gleams with borrowed lamplight, and a gentleman's hat rolls past your feet, empty, its owner nowhere within sight along the whole rain slicked street. Behind it comes a low chuckle from somewhere deep in the wet dark, patient and entirely unhurried.

You do not bother chasing the hat, because chasing it is clearly the point of leaving it there in the first place. You watch the alley mouth instead, where a shape with backward hands is already stepping calmly out to meet you, tiger's face arranged into an expression of polite, practiced surprise at your restraint. It compliments you on not taking the bait. You answer by raising your weapon instead of accepting the compliment gracefully.

A guild seal lies snapped clean in half on the wet cobbles, its wax still faintly warm, whoever broke it barely gone from sight around the nearest corner. The fog around the alley mouth smells thick of hot fur and old honey, a combination that has come to mean exactly one thing in this part of the city.

Somewhere close a voice hums a tune that is almost a lullaby and entirely, unmistakably a threat dressed up in something gentler. You follow the hum past shuttered stalls and a sagging guild banner until it resolves into a shape with a tiger's face and a courtier's folded hands, waiting beneath a lamppost as though it had all the patience in the world to spare on you tonight. You do not return the tune. You draw your weapon and let the humming stop on its own.

The lamplight throws two shadows from a single shape crouched on the gutter's edge, one distinctly human, one very much not, the two of them seeming to argue silently over which face ought to be worn for the occasion of your arrival. Fog curls thick around the disagreement, muffling whatever quiet sounds it makes.

You do not wait around for it to decide. You close the gap while it is still busy being two things at once, weapon raised before either shadow has settled into something solid enough to fight back properly. It notices you a beat too late, backward hands only halfway folded into a greeting, tiger's face still half formed beneath the human one. Whatever advantage indecision cost it, you intend to spend before it gets the chance to recover.

A backward handprint presses wet into the fogged glass of a shopfront window, five fingers splayed the wrong direction from any human wrist, pressed there recently enough that condensation is still creeping back in around its edges. Whatever left it is still close enough to watch you notice, patient and clearly entertained by your reaction.

You back slowly toward open street, keeping your weapon raised the entire way, refusing to turn your back on the handprint or whatever left it behind. A chuckle drifts out of the fog, warm as honey, complimenting your caution in a voice that makes the compliment feel like an insult. You do not answer. You simply wait for the shape to finally step fully into the open, where you can meet it on your own terms instead of its.

Fog rolls thick down the crooked lane behind the guild district, pooling around lampposts already half drowned in mist, and somewhere within it a cat yowls once before the sound cuts off far too abruptly to be natural. You slow your pace, weapon half raised, listening for whatever silenced it so cleanly.

A shape steps out from between two shuttered stalls, wearing a gentleman's coat over a face that shifts, tiger one moment, something almost human the next, as though it has not quite decided which mask suits tonight's meeting. Its hands fold backward at the wrist in a gesture of welcome that chills more than it charms. It offers pleasant words, honey smooth and entirely hollow. You answer with steel, and the fog around you seems, just slightly, to draw back to watch.

The fog over this quarter of the city never fully lifts, not even at noon, and by lamplight it turns every alley into a corridor with no visible end. You have been following the smell of hot fur and old honey for the better part of an hour, past shuttered guild stalls and a chestnut cart abandoned mid sale, its coals still faintly glowing under a fresh dusting of ash as though whoever tipped it walked away only moments ago.

The trail leads you to a courtyard behind the tanners' guild, where a decanter of dark liquor sits untouched on an overturned barrel, offered up like a peace treaty nobody actually signed. You do not touch it. Somewhere close, eyes catch the lamplight wrong, too bright and too golden, watching from just beyond the reach of the light with the patience of something that has never once had to work for its dinner.

When the shape finally steps forward, it wears a gentleman's coat over a tiger's face, an arrangement that should look absurd and instead belongs perfectly here, on this rotten and beautiful street. Its hands fold backward at the wrist in a courtier's bow, and it offers you terms in a voice like honey poured slow over gravel. You do not accept the bow, and you do not accept the terms. You draw your weapon instead, and somewhere in the fog behind you, something that has been circling this whole time finally, softly, begins to purr.

Rooftops crowd so close together over this stretch of the city that the sky between them narrows to a single grey seam, fog pooling along its edges like smoke with nowhere else to go. You climb because the street below has gone unnervingly quiet, the particular quiet that settles right before something very large decides to move, and because a fire escape at least offers you a clear line of sight in every direction that matters.

At the top, a shape crouches easily on the narrow ledge, entirely unbothered by the height or the rain, a tiger's face turned toward you with an expression that might be curiosity and might simply be hunger wearing a very patient mask. It rises slowly into a gentleman's posture, brushing invisible dust from a coat that somehow stays perfectly dry despite the weather, and offers a bow so practiced it could only have been performed a thousand times before, for a thousand other guests who never left this rooftop the same way they arrived.

You grip the wet rail and refuse to look away first, because on a ledge this narrow, looking away is the only mistake that actually matters. It seems to appreciate your composure, tilting its head with something almost like respect, before its tail flicks once, testing the distance between your footing and its own. You do not wait to see what the flick decides. You close the gap yourself, weapon raised, before the choice is made for you by claws instead of conversation.

The sewer stair behind the guildhall breathes warm, wrong air up into the cold night, the kind of warmth that has no business rising from anything as foul as sewage. You go down anyway, one hand trailing the slick brick, boots finding the worn centers of steps countless other feet have used before yours, some of them, you suspect, never coming back up to use them again.

At the bottom, a lantern burns behind an iron grate, and behind that grate a shape sits with its knees drawn up, hands folded backward at the wrist in an attitude of patient, courtly waiting. It watches you arrive without surprise, as though your arrival were simply the next item on an evening's itinerary it had already planned in careful detail. It speaks first, voice honey smooth, offering safe passage back to the surface if you simply turn around now and forget this entire visit ever happened.

You do not answer, and you do not turn around. The lantern gutters at your silence, as if the thing behind the grate had genuinely hoped you would take the offer and spare it the trouble of a fight. When you raise your weapon instead, its tiger's mouth curls into something that might be disappointment and might be delight, the two expressions apparently sharing the same shape on a face like that. Either way, the grate begins, very slowly, to bend.

Fog rolls off the river and pools thick in the alley behind the fishmongers' stalls, carrying brine and something else beneath it, warm and wrong and steadily closer. A cracked mirror leans against a crate someone left out for collection, and in its glass your reflection stands a half second behind where you actually are, as though it is still catching up to your movements one beat too late for comfort.

You do not trust the mirror, and you are right not to. A tiger's shadow slides across the glass behind your reflection, slow and unhurried, though the alley at your back shows nothing at all when your nerve finally breaks and you risk a glance over your shoulder. Somewhere close a low chuckle rides the fog, delighted, entirely unbothered that you noticed the trick. It seems, if anything, pleased that you are paying attention at last.

You turn your back on the mirror entirely and face the alley instead, weapon raised, because whatever this is clearly wants your eyes fixed on the wrong thing while the real danger closes from elsewhere. The shape resolves out of the fog a moment later, gentleman's coat, tiger's face, backward hands folded in a bow that manages to look both gracious and mocking at once. You do not return the bow. You answer with steel, and the mirror behind you finally, quietly, cracks the rest of the way through.

The guild seal on the courtyard door has been snapped clean in half, its wax still faintly warm, and beyond the broken lock the hall sits dark except for a single guttering candle someone left burning on purpose, as if to make certain you would find your way in rather than turn back at the threshold. Coins are scattered across the floor in a trail too neat to be an accident, each one placed with the precision of a lure rather than the carelessness of a robbery.

You follow the coins because standing in the dark waiting for whatever scattered them to come find you first is a worse plan by every measure you can think of. The trail leads past the guildmaster's overturned chair to a shape rising slowly from behind it, backward hands folding into a courtly greeting, a tiger's face arranging itself into something almost like an apology. It is not sorry, not remotely, and you can tell by the way it keeps smiling even as it speaks the words.

It calls the coins a small hospitality, an honest gesture of goodwill before the real business begins. Honest is the last word this thing has earned tonight, and you tell it so with your weapon rather than your voice, stepping past every coin without slowing. The candle gutters as you move, throwing the shape's shadow twice as tall across the guildhall wall, backward hands stretching wide before you close the distance yourself.

Rain slicks the cobbles until every surface in the tanners' quarter gleams with borrowed lamplight, and the alley behind the tannery reeks of curing hide and hot fur in a combination thick enough to turn your stomach before you have even glimpsed the source. Claw marks score the doorframe ahead at eye height, four parallel gouges deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, as though something paused to measure a previous visitor for a fitting it never finished.

You step past the marks anyway, because turning back means admitting the fog has already decided how tonight goes. Behind the door, low and pleased, something purrs at the sound of your footsteps, utterly certain of its welcome. When the door swings open on its own, unlatched from within, a gentleman's silhouette stands waiting in the lamplight, backward hands already folded, offering a bow you have no intention of returning in kind.

It thanks you for coming so promptly, as though you ever had any real choice in the matter once the claw marks pointed the way. You answer by raising your weapon instead of a greeting, and the purring behind it deepens into something closer to a growl, low and delighted, the sound of a very patient predator finally getting to skip the pleasantries and move straight to the part of the evening it actually came for.

Between one guttering lamppost and the next, the fog thickens into something with a shape, reversed hands folding into a courtier's bow so smooth it could only have been practiced for centuries of evenings exactly like this one. It speaks your name wrong on purpose, testing the exact shape of your flinch the way a tailor tests the fit of a hem before committing to the final stitch.

The cobbles underfoot are slick with old rain and older grime, and you plant your feet carefully before answering with steel instead of words, because words are clearly the game it wants to play and steel is the only language you intend to speak tonight. It does not seem surprised by your silence; if anything, the tiger's mouth curls a little wider, as though silence was the exact answer it had been hoping for all along, better than anything you could have said.

Somewhere behind it, a second lamppost gutters out entirely on its own, plunging half the alley into a dark that feels suddenly, deliberately, much larger than it was only a moment before. You do not let the darkness rattle you. You close the distance instead, weapon first, refusing to give this thing the reaction its theatre so clearly wants, and somewhere in that widening dark, its bow finally, slowly, begins to straighten back up into something ready to fight.

A cracked shop window along the tanners' row shows a reflection standing just behind where you actually are, though the street at your back proves entirely, verifiably empty the moment your nerve finally breaks and you risk a glance to check. You do not look back a second time, because the first glance already told you everything you needed to know about how this evening intends to go.

You watch the glass instead, counting your own heartbeat against whatever rhythm the reflection is keeping, patient and unhurried in a way that makes the patience itself feel like a threat. The tiger's face in the glass smiles a half second before your own reflection does, patient as a debt collector who already knows perfectly well you cannot pay whatever this costs. You strike the moment the reflection's hand moves first, shattering the window in a spray of glass that sounds far more satisfying than your own shaking hand really has any right to feel.

Behind you, in the real dark, something laughs low and warm, delighted that you finally decided to stop merely watching and start actually fighting. The shape steps out of the shadow a heartbeat later, gentleman's coat, backward hands, a courtier's bow offered as though the broken window were an invitation rather than the opening move of a fight it very much intends to finish on its own terms.

The tannery alley behind the guild district reeks of curing hide, hot fur, and old rain all tangled together into a combination that has come to mean exactly one thing in this part of the city, and you have learned by now to trust that particular smell more than almost anything else your eyes can tell you. Claw marks score the doorframe ahead at precisely eye height, deep enough to catch a careless boot heel, as if measuring some earlier visitor for a fitting they never survived to collect.

Somewhere close a voice hums a tune that is almost a lullaby and entirely, unmistakably a threat dressed up in something gentler, the kind of tune meant to make you lower your guard exactly long enough for a claw to find the gap. You follow the hum past shuttered stalls and a sagging guild banner heavy with rain until it resolves into a shape with a tiger's face and a courtier's folded hands, waiting beneath a dead lamppost as though it had all the patience in the world to spare on you tonight.

You do not return the tune, and you do not lower your guard. You draw your weapon instead and let the humming stop entirely on its own, cut short mid note the instant the shape realizes its lullaby has failed to do the one job it was meant to do. What follows is considerably less musical, and considerably more honest about what this alley has actually been promising you since the very first claw mark.

A guild apprentice's satchel lies open on the wet cobbles, its contents perfectly undisturbed, coins and a stub of chalk and a half eaten chestnut all arranged as if someone wanted you to stop and take careful inventory before continuing any further down the alley. You do not stop for long, because something clearly wanted your attention fixed here, and that alone is reason enough to keep moving toward whatever it was hoping to distract you from.

The true trail lies beyond it, marked by claw gouges scored deep into the brick and a smell of hot fur growing steadily stronger with every step you take past the abandoned satchel. At the alley's end, a gentleman's silhouette waits beneath a dead lamppost, backward hands already folded, smiling the exact patient smile of someone who left the satchel there on purpose and is thoroughly delighted that you noticed the bait without ever actually pausing to take it.

It compliments your instincts in a voice like honey poured slow over gravel, the kind of compliment designed to soften you up before the real conversation even starts. You do not accept the compliment any more than you accepted the satchel, and you answer instead with your weapon raised and ready, because whatever conversation this thing was hoping to have, you have already decided tonight is going to be considerably shorter and considerably sharper than it planned for.

Fog swallows the mouth of the sewer stair whole, thick enough that the lamplight behind you barely reaches past your own shoulder before it gives up entirely and lets the dark take over. From somewhere down in that dark comes a low, satisfied purr, unhurried and confident, the exact sound of something that already believes tonight's hunt is as good as finished before it has even properly begun in earnest.

You start down the stairs anyway, one careful step at a time, letting your eyes adjust slowly to the deepening dark rather than rushing blind into whatever is waiting below. At the bottom, a shape rises out of a puddle of stagnant water, tiger faced and gentleman dressed, backward hands spreading wide in a mocking little bow of welcome that somehow manages to feel both gracious and threatening in the exact same gesture.

It compliments your nerve in that same honey smooth voice, clearly expecting the compliment to unsettle you far more thoroughly than its claws ever could. You raise your weapon instead of trembling, refusing to give it the reaction it was so obviously hoping for, and for just one brief moment its confident smile flickers, the first genuine crack you have seen in all that careful, practiced composure since you first came down these stairs.

The guild banner above the courtyard hangs sodden and heavy with rain, dripping steadily onto cobbles already slick from the storm that passed through an hour ago and left the whole quarter smelling of wet stone and distant lightning. Paw prints track across the gravel toward a shuttered side door, each print the size of a dinner plate and pressed deep enough into the mud to hold standing water long after the rain itself has moved on elsewhere.

You follow the prints because the alternative is waiting here in the open, and open ground clearly favors whatever left tracks that large without any apparent effort. Behind the shuttered door something purrs, low and pleased, entirely certain of its welcome, the unmistakable sound of a predator that has never once had to work very hard for its supper in a city this rotten and this generous with easy prey.

You test the door and find it unlocked, swinging open on oiled hinges someone has clearly bothered to maintain, which tells you this particular welcome has been prepared for far longer than just tonight. Beyond it, lamplight throws a gentleman's shadow across a room arranged with an unsettling kind of care, backward hands already folding into greeting before you have even fully crossed the threshold, and you answer that greeting with your weapon raised instead of your voice.

Steam hisses continuously from a broken grate as you cross the narrow alley, fog parting just long enough to show two hands folded in what looks, at a distance, like polite greeting, fingers bent backward at every knuckle in a way that turns the greeting into something closer to a threat dressed up carefully in manners. The face beneath the hood is almost kind, almost human, almost, disturbingly, almost your own, as though it borrowed the expression specifically to unsettle you.

You keep both eyes fixed firmly on the claws and neither on the smile, because the smile is so obviously meant to be the distraction here. It speaks in a voice like honey poured slow over gravel, offering terms you have absolutely no intention of considering no matter how reasonably they are phrased. Somewhere behind it a second shape flickers briefly into being and out again, a trick of fog and lamplight, or perhaps not a trick at all, you genuinely cannot tell which and do not much like either answer.

You do not wait to find out for certain. You close the distance instead, weapon leading, because whatever advantage this thing gains from being watched too closely is an advantage you have no intention of handing over for free. The claws move first, faster than the smile ever promised they would, and you are already moving to meet them before the fog can decide which shape it wants to commit to next.

The gravel path behind the guildhall glitters with scattered coins under the lamplight, each one placed with the precision of a lure rather than the carelessness of an accident, the whole trail leading straight toward a shadowed archway where the fog pools thickest and lingers longest. Somewhere within that shadow, something waits with the exact patience of a debt collector counting interest on a debt you never even knew you owed.

You step past every coin without slowing your pace, because stopping to collect any of them is precisely what this trail wants you to do, and you have learned by now not to give this city what it wants without a fight first. At the archway a shape steps forward to meet you, backward hands folded, tiger's face arranged into a courtier's polite and entirely practiced smile that somehow manages to look both welcoming and hungry at the very same time.

It calls the scattered coins a small hospitality, a gesture of goodwill before the real business of the evening properly begins in earnest. You answer by drawing your weapon rather than accepting the gesture, and the polite smile widens abruptly into something with entirely too many teeth to still be called goodwill by any honest measure. Whatever business it had planned, you intend to make considerably shorter and considerably less pleasant than it was hoping for.

A cat yowls once somewhere above the rooftops and cuts off mid cry, and the silence that follows presses down far heavier than the sound itself ever did, thick enough that you can hear your own pulse pounding steadily in your ears as you stand still and listen. You climb the fire escape two rungs at a time anyway, fog curling cold around your boots with every single step upward toward whatever silenced that cry so cleanly.

At the top, a tiger's grin waits patiently in the lamplight, entirely too pleased that you actually bothered coming up to look for whatever made that sound in the first place. It sits with a gentleman's posture, backward hands folded neatly in its lap, as though it has all the time in the world to spare and knows perfectly well that you do not share that particular luxury tonight.

You raise your weapon and refuse to let the silence, or the grin, or the careful patience decide how this next moment unfolds between the two of you. It rises unhurried to meet you, tail flicking once in something between greeting and challenge, and the rooftop beneath you both suddenly feels a great deal smaller and a great deal more dangerous than it did from the ground below only moments earlier.

The sewer entrance behind the fish market breathes out warm, wrong air that has no business rising from anything as cold and foul as sewage, and painted crudely on the brick above it is a sigil someone scratched in a visible hurry, clearly meant as more warning than welcome for anyone paying attention. The door beside it hangs deliberately ajar, an invitation dressed up as an accident.

Somewhere below, a voice like honey poured slow over gravel calls up an invitation you have absolutely no intention of accepting on its own terms, no matter how pleasantly it is phrased from down in the dark. You go down anyway, weapon leading the way, boots finding the worn centers of steps that countless other feet have used long before yours, some of them, you suspect, never bothering to climb back up again once they reached the bottom.

At the bottom, a shape waits with a tiger's face and a gentleman's carefully folded hands, delighted that you actually came down to meet it and visibly disappointed that you came armed and entirely unwilling to talk things over first. It offers one last bargain anyway, out of habit more than hope, and you answer it the same way you answered the sigil on the brick above: not with words, but with steel raised and ready to finish this properly.

Rain slicks the cobbles outside the guildhall until every surface gleams with borrowed lamplight, and a gentleman's hat rolls past your feet, empty, its owner nowhere within sight along the entire rain slicked street stretching out ahead of you. Behind it comes a low chuckle from somewhere deep in the wet dark, patient and entirely unhurried, clearly enjoying whatever reaction the hat was meant to provoke.

You do not bother chasing the hat, because chasing it is obviously the entire point of leaving it there in your path in the first place, a lure dressed up as an accident. You watch the alley mouth instead, where a shape with backward hands is already stepping calmly out to meet you, tiger's face arranged into an expression of polite, practiced surprise at your restraint in not taking the bait it left behind.

It compliments you on resisting the hat, clearly disappointed that its little trick failed to work the way it usually does on less careful visitors to this street. You answer the compliment by raising your weapon instead of accepting it gracefully, and the chuckle behind the shape deepens into something with far less patience and far more teeth than it was showing only a moment before, the pleasantries finally giving way to the fight it actually came here for.

The lamplight throws two shadows from a single shape crouched on the gutter's edge, one distinctly human, one very much not, the two of them seeming to argue silently over which face ought to be worn for the occasion of your sudden arrival. Fog curls thick around the disagreement, muffling whatever quiet sounds it makes as the two shadows shift and settle against each other.

You do not wait around for it to decide which face wins. You close the gap while it is still busy being two things at once, weapon raised before either shadow has settled into something solid enough to properly fight back. It notices you a beat too late, backward hands only halfway folded into a greeting, tiger's face still half formed beneath the human one it was apparently still deciding whether to keep.

Whatever advantage its indecision cost it, you fully intend to spend before it gets any chance to recover its composure.
