The two shadows finally merge into one as your weapon comes down, the human mask dropping away entirely to reveal the tiger beneath it in full, no longer bothering to argue with itself about which face the evening actually called for.

A backward handprint presses wet into the fogged glass of a shopfront window, five fingers splayed the wrong direction from any human wrist, pressed there recently enough that condensation is still visibly creeping back in around its edges as you lean close to examine it. Whatever left it is still close enough to watch you notice, patient and clearly entertained by whatever reaction crosses your face in that moment.

You back slowly toward open street, keeping your weapon raised the entire way, refusing to turn your back on the handprint or on whatever left it behind in the fogged glass. A chuckle drifts out of the surrounding fog, warm as honey, complimenting your caution in a voice that somehow makes the compliment feel like an insult dressed up as praise.

You do not answer the chuckle. You simply wait for the shape to finally step fully into the open, where you can meet it properly on your own terms instead of arguing with a voice hidden somewhere in the fog. When it does step out, gentleman's coat and tiger's face and backward hands all arranged in careful, practiced welcome, you are already moving before it finishes its first pleasant word of greeting.

A guild seal lies snapped clean in half on the wet cobbles outside the courtyard gate, its wax still faintly warm, whoever broke it barely gone from sight around the nearest corner when you arrive to find the damage. The fog around the alley mouth smells thick of hot fur and old honey, a combination that has come to mean exactly one thing to anyone who has spent enough time in this part of the city to recognize it.

You follow that smell past shuttered stalls and a sagging guild banner heavy with rain until it resolves into a shape waiting beneath a dead lamppost, a tiger's face and a courtier's folded hands arranged with the patience of something that has all the time in the world to spare on a single evening's hunt. It bows before you can speak, deep and theatrical, the kind of bow that assumes an audience rather than actually earning one through any real courtesy.

You do not return the bow, and you do not wait for it to finish straightening back up. You draw your weapon while it is still mid gesture, and the smell of hot fur and old honey sharpens sudden and sharp in the fog around you both, the unmistakable scent of a performance abruptly cut short in favor of the fight it was always actually building toward from the very first snapped seal.

The alley narrows steadily until your shoulders nearly brush both walls at once, funneling you toward a single crooked door marked with a symbol scratched fresh into old wood, the paint of it still faintly wet to the touch when you pause to examine the mark more closely. Behind the door something purrs, content and thoroughly unhurried, utterly certain that whatever comes through will prove entirely worth the wait it has already spent.

You test the handle and it turns without the slightest resistance, oiled and ready in a way that alone tells you everything you need to know before you ever actually step through into whatever waits beyond it. Inside, lamplight throws a gentleman's shadow twice as tall as the room should reasonably allow, backward hands already folded in a greeting that manages to look both welcoming and vaguely threatening at once.

It thanks you for coming so promptly, as though you had any real choice in the matter after following claw marks and honey scented fog this far down a narrowing alley. You answer by raising your weapon instead of returning the courtesy it so clearly expects, and the purring behind the shadow deepens at once into something with considerably sharper edges, the patient welcome finally giving way to the fight that was always waiting underneath it.

A guild lantern above the alley gutters and relights itself twice in a row, far too deliberate to be wind, the flame flickering in a rhythm that feels almost like a signal from something out there that wants very badly to be noticed before it ever shows itself directly. You keep walking anyway, boots loud and deliberate on the wet stone, refusing to let flickering light dictate the pace of your approach through this narrow, rotten stretch of the city.

At the alley's end the lantern finally steadies, and in its restored glow a shape waits with a gentleman's posture, a tiger's face, and hands folded backward at the wrist in patient, practiced welcome that has clearly been rehearsed for exactly this kind of arrival. It compliments your composure, visibly disappointed that the flickering game failed to rattle you more thoroughly than it evidently hoped it would.

You do not answer the compliment. You close the distance instead, weapon raised, while the lantern behind you burns steady at last, as if it too has finally decided the games are over and the real business of the evening can properly begin between the two of you. The shape's smile does not waver, but its stance shifts, just slightly, into something readier for what comes next.

The bell tower has not rung in a hundred years, and the rope is black with rot when you climb past it into the belfry. Fog pours through the broken slats like something poured from a jug, and in the middle of the floor the Vampire Umbral Lord waits with his coat hanging open, unbothered by the cold. He does not raise his voice. He simply says that the city forgot how to lock its doors, and that forgetting is an invitation. Bats stream out from under the eaves, a furious black weather all their own, and the fight begins before you are ready for it, the way real fights always do.

His claws cut faster than a blade should be able to, four long lines opening in the air where a blow only grazed you, and for a moment you realize how outmatched you are up here with the wind screaming through broken glass. He laughs, low, and tells you that every hero who climbed this tower is still down in the crypt below it, filed away neat as coins. You do not answer. You watch his shadow instead of his hands, because the shadow moves a half-beat before he does, and that half-beat is the whole fight.

DC 15 or the fear takes your knees out from under you, and the first time you fail that save you feel it, ice up your spine, your grip gone soft on the hilt. But you make the second save, and the third, and each one steadies you a little more, until his shadow lunges and you are already turning, already swinging where he will be rather than where he was.

The blow lands clean, and instead of blood there is only a long exhale, like a candle being smothered rather than blown out. He does not fall so much as fold, the coat settling empty over the floorboards, the bats scattering to the four corners of the night. The bell above you shivers once, a low true note nobody rang, as if the tower itself is glad to be finished with him.

Rain comes down the gutters in sheets by the time you find the guild hall he has claimed for his own, its sign long since stolen, its door standing open like a mouth. Inside, candles burn blue instead of gold, and the air tastes like pennies. The Vampire Umbral Lord sits on a throne built from stacked ledgers and unpaid debts, and he rises without hurry, telling you that this city already belongs to him, has belonged to him since before you were born, and that you are only the latest fee it owes.

You do not waste time on words. He crosses the room faster than anything wearing a coat should move, and the first exchange is close and ugly, blade against claw, sparks lighting the dark for half a heartbeat at a time. He gets under your guard once, and the cold of it goes all the way to bone, colder than any winter you have felt, a cold that seems to want something from you rather than just hurt you.

Roll damage: 2d6+3, and it barely slows him, because he heals what he drains, feeding on every drop the fight costs you. That is the trick of him, you understand now, watching him straighten after a hit that should have dropped him twice over. You have to make him bleed faster than he can drink, and that means pressing when every instinct says to retreat.

So you press. You put everything into the next exchange, every point of strength you have, and this time when steel finds him it finds him true, opening something that does not close again. He staggers back into his own stacked ledgers, and they collapse under him in a soft avalanche of paper, and the candles guttering blue all around the hall turn gold again, as if the whole room had been holding its breath for years and only now remembers how to let it out.

The canal water is black and still under the footbridge where the Vampire Umbral Lord waits, standing on the surface of it like the water is stone, his reflection wrong in a way you cannot name until you notice it has no reflection at all. Fog drifts off the water in slow curtains, and somewhere behind you a lamp goes out, then another, until the only light left is whatever you brought with you.

He tells you, almost gently, that he has counted every hero who tried this bridge, and that the number is not small. You do not answer him. You step onto the bridge instead, and the boards groan, and his smile widens like he has been waiting a long time for someone foolish enough to accept the invitation.

He moves like smoke poured into the shape of a man, and the first strike he lands folds you half over the rail, the cold of it spreading through your chest before the pain even arrives. You catch yourself before you go into the canal, barely, fingers white on wet wood, and you understand that going into that water would be the last mistake you ever made.

Initiative rolled again in your head, faster this time, and you meet his next lunge with everything you have left, blade up and through, and for once he does not simply absorb it. Something in him cracks, a sound like ice breaking on a pond in the first thaw, and he recoils across the bridge with his coat smoking at the shoulder.

You do not let him recover. You close the distance he is trying to build and finish it there on the boards, and he goes down without ceremony, without a final speech, just a long unravelling into fog that the wind carries off the bridge and out over the black water, gone before it even touches the surface.

The vault door hangs open when you find it, torn off one hinge like paper, and stolen coin spills across the threshold in a glowing river that never quite stops moving. Somewhere in the dark past it, the Vampire Umbral Lord is counting, actually counting, coin by coin, and he does not stop when you enter, only says that a job this size deserves attention, and that you have his, now.

He rises from the pile without disturbing it, coat folding around him like wings settling, and crosses the vault faster than the torchlight can follow. The first hit takes your breath clean out of you, a cold so total it feels like falling through ice into black water, and you stagger back into a stack of barrels that were never meant to hold your weight.

You realize, gasping, that he is not trying to kill you first. He is trying to frighten you into running, because a fear-frozen hero is easier prey than a fighting one. DC 15, and you fail it once, your knees going soft, your hands shaking around the hilt. But the second save holds, and something in you goes hard and calm at the same time, like a coin cooling from molten to solid.

You come off the barrels with everything you have, and this time he does not expect it, does not expect the fear to have burned off into something worse for him. Steel meets him true, and he staggers back into his own hoard, coins scattering, glowing river disturbed at last.

He does not get up smiling this time. He unravels there among the coin, thin as smoke, and the vault door creaks on its one hinge as if the whole building is exhaling, and the coins settle slow and quiet, and finally, properly, still. You stand there a long moment before you trust your legs enough to walk back out into the ordinary dark of the street, letting the fee this job asked of you finally feel worth it.

Nineteen steps down into the sewer and the air changes, goes thick and mineral, and the lamplight from your torch catches on things that should not be shining down here, rings and buckles and buttons stripped off travelers who never came back up. The Vampire Umbral Lord waits at the bottom, perched on a ledge above the sluice, telling you the tunnels below this city are older than the towers above it, and hungrier.

He drops without a sound, landing between you and the way back up, and the fight starts with him already inside your guard, claws finding the gap between shoulder and collar before you can turn. The cold of it runs down your whole arm, and for a moment your grip on the hilt goes numb and stupid, fingers refusing to close.

You shift the blade to your other hand out of pure stubbornness, and that surprises him enough to buy you half a second, which turns out to be exactly the half second the fight needed. He recoils from the returning cut, more startled than hurt, coat catching a thin line of torchlight through the tear it leaves.

Roll damage again, and again, matching him blow for blow now that the shock of the ambush has worn off, and the sewer rings with the sound of it, steel on stone, steel on nothing at all when he dissolves half a step from every strike, only to reform a breath later, always a beat behind now instead of ahead.

The last hit catches him fully reformed, no half step left to dodge into, and he goes down hard against the sluice gate, water rushing past him carrying whatever remains once the fog of him lifts off the tunnel floor and drifts up toward the grates, toward the ordinary night above the city. You climb the nineteen steps back up slower than you came down them, torch guttering low, and the mineral smell fades behind you with every stair, until the ordinary rot of the street feels almost sweet by comparison.

The rooftops above the docks are slick with fog when you catch up to him, three towers apart from the nearest ladder down, the whole city spread out below like a map somebody spilled ink on. The Vampire Umbral Lord stands at the roof's edge with his coat snapping in the wind, and he tells you, almost bored, that heroes always find him eventually, and that it never changes anything.

He comes at you across the tiles faster than a man his size should move, and the first pass nearly puts you off the edge entirely, only a fistful of gutter iron keeping you from a very long fall. You haul yourself back up in time to meet the second pass, and this one you are ready for, blade already rising.

The exchange that follows is close and fast and entirely without mercy, both of you trading ground back and forth across roof tiles that crack and shift underfoot, and twice you feel his cold reach past your guard and twice you shrug it off through sheer refusal to fall. Somewhere below, church bells you cannot see start ringing the hour, oddly calm against the chaos up here.

You use the ringing as cover, timing your next strike to the peal of it, and it catches him mid turn, off balance for the first time since the fight began. He staggers toward the roof's edge himself now, coat snapping wrong, footing gone.

He does not fall so much as let go, unravelling into fog before he ever hits the street below, and the fog scatters out over the rooftops and down into the fog bank already sitting over the docks, indistinguishable from it within a few seconds, gone the way smoke goes when a very old fire is finally, finally out. You sit down right there on the cracked tiles for a moment, breathing hard, letting the church bells finish their hour before you trust your legs to find the ladder down.

The tea house has been closed for a hundred years, its windows boarded, its sign faded past reading, but light leaks gold through the cracks anyway, and that is how you know the Vampire Umbral Lord has made it his. Inside, dust hangs undisturbed except around the low table where he sits, and he gestures you toward the seat across from him like the fight has not already started the moment you crossed his threshold.

You do not sit. You draw instead, and he sighs like you have disappointed him, rising from the table without upsetting a single dust mote, coat settling around him as he crosses the small room in two strides that should not have covered that much ground. The first exchange happens right there among the low furniture, blade and claw tangled close, nowhere to retreat to.

He gets a hand around your collar at one point, cold flooding down through the fabric fast enough to steal your breath, and for a heartbeat you are sure this is how it ends, right here among somebody's abandoned porcelain. But you drive an elbow back and he lets go, more surprised than hurt, and that surprise is the opening you needed.

You press it hard, driving him back into the boarded window, splinters raining down around both of you as old wood finally gives way to violence it was built to keep out. He gets one clawed hand up to block and it is not enough.

The blow that finishes him sends him back through the broken window and into the alley beyond, and by the time you climb through after him there is nothing left but coat and fog, drifting off between the buildings, and the gold light in the tea house finally goes out for good. You step back inside once, just to be sure, and find only dust again, undisturbed, as if a hundred years had simply resumed exactly where they left off.

The pawnshop window is full of things nobody ever came back to claim, rings and lockets and a longsword nobody in this city could afford, and behind the counter where a clerk should stand the Vampire Umbral Lord counts silver instead, coin by coin, into folds of black cloth. He does not look up right away. He tells you that everything in this shop has a story, and that yours will fit right in beside the others.

You step through the door anyway, past the bell that does not ring, and he finally looks up, unhurried, setting the silver aside like you are an interruption worth exactly one glance. Then he is across the counter in an instant, faster than the space should allow, claws finding your shoulder before your blade even clears its sheath.

The cold spreads fast, down your arm and into your chest, and for a moment the whole shop tilts sideways, lockets and rings blurring together into one glowing mess. You force your grip closed anyway, stubborn past the point of sense, and swing wide rather than precise, more instinct than aim.

It connects better than you had any right to expect, catching him across the ribs as he turns back toward the counter, and he staggers into his own shelves, rings and lockets raining down around both of you in a bright cascade. He recoils, more startled than truly hurt, coat torn at the side.

You press before he can reset, driving him back against the pawnshop's rear wall, and the final blow lands clean through the tear you already opened. He goes to smoke right there among the fallen jewelry, and the bell above the door rings once on its own, very softly, as if the shop itself is glad to finally be rid of its worst customer. You gather what you can carry of the scattered rings on your way out, figuring their owners are long past claiming them, and the street outside feels ordinary again for the first time in what felt like hours.

Snow does not fall often in this city, but it is falling now, drifting past broken lampposts as you climb the last flight of stairs to the tower's crown, where the Vampire Umbral Lord stands with his back to you, looking out over rooftops gone white and quiet. He speaks without turning, telling you that snow always makes the hunting easier, because it shows every footstep a hero makes.

You do not bother trying to be quiet after that. He turns as you close the distance, and the fight opens fast, claws meeting blade in the thin cold air atop the tower, both of you breathing white clouds into the falling snow. He is stronger up close than his coat suggests, strong enough to nearly take your feet out from under you on the icy stone.

You slide instead of falling, using the ice rather than fighting it, and come up under his guard from an angle he did not expect, steel finding the gap between ribs and coat. He recoils with a sound more surprise than pain, backing toward the tower's edge where the snow has drifted deepest.

He tries one more rush, desperate now, and you meet it square, refusing to give ground on the ice even as your boots start to slip. The exchange is close, closer than any before it, but your blade finds true one last time, driving deep enough that even he cannot shrug it off.

He goes down into the snowdrift at the tower's edge, and the fog of him rises pale against the falling white, indistinguishable from the snow within moments, and by the time you catch your breath the only mark he left behind is a coat, already filling up with quiet, endless snow. You stand there a while longer than you need to, letting the cold air clear your head, watching the city below turn slowly, peacefully white.

The market square empties fast once word gets around that something is wrong tonight, stalls abandoned mid-count, coin left scattered on tables nobody stopped to collect. In the center of it, where the fountain used to run before the frost took it, the Vampire Umbral Lord stands perfectly still, and he tells you, without turning, that fear moves through a crowd faster than any illness, and that he has always found that useful.

You cross the empty square toward him anyway, boots loud on stone nobody else dares walk, and he finally turns to face you, coat settling around him like something folding its wings. The fight opens fast and mean, right there among the abandoned stalls, produce crushed underfoot as you trade ground back and forth across the square.

He knocks you into a stack of crates at one point, hard enough that stars swim briefly at the edge of your vision, and you feel the cold reach for you again while you are still finding your feet. You get the blade up just in time, more reflex than plan, and it is enough to buy the half second you need to breathe.

That half second turns the fight. You come off the crates faster than he expects, closing the distance instead of retreating from it, and the blow you land opens something in him that his usual recovery cannot quite close fast enough. He staggers back toward the frozen fountain, coat torn, breath ragged in a way that should not be possible for something that does not breathe.

The last strike sends him down onto the fountain's icy rim, and he unravels there into thin fog that drifts up and out over the abandoned stalls, gone before the first frightened stall keeper works up the nerve to peek back out from wherever they were hiding.

The lighthouse at the harbor's mouth has not thrown a beam in years, its lamp room dark, its stairs spiraling up into a silence that feels wrong the higher you climb. At the top, where the great lens should be turning, the Vampire Umbral Lord stands instead, and he tells you that ships have been finding this coast by luck alone for a decade, and that he prefers it that way.

You draw your blade the moment you clear the last stair, and he is already moving, crossing the small round room faster than its size should allow. The first exchange happens right against the broken lens itself, glass fragments catching what little moonlight leaks through the shutters, throwing strange colored light across both of you as you trade blows.

He gets past your guard once, cold spreading through your shoulder fast enough to numb your whole arm, and for a moment you fumble the blade badly enough that it nearly slips from your grip entirely. You catch it left-handed instead, awkward but functional, and that awkwardness turns out to confuse him just long enough.

You press the confusion, driving him back against the broken lens, glass groaning under the pressure of his weight. He tries to slip sideways and you do not let him, following the movement instead of losing it, and the blade finds him clean through the gap his own retreat opened.

He goes down among the broken glass, unravelling into fog that drifts strangely, catching what light there is and scattering it rainbow-colored across the walls for just a moment before dispersing entirely out through the shutters and off over the dark water below. You pick your way back down the spiral stairs slowly, glass crunching underfoot, already wondering whether anyone will ever bother relighting that lamp now that its keeper is finally gone.

Smoke still hangs over the burned block where the fire brigade gave up hours ago, and it is there, among the blackened beams of a house nobody will rebuild, that the Vampire Umbral Lord waits for you, entirely untouched by the heat that scorched everything around him. He tells you the fire was not his doing, though he does not mind it, and that ruined places always suit him better than whole ones.

You step over a collapsed beam to reach him, ash stirring under your boots, and he meets you halfway, faster than the wreckage should let anything move. The first exchange happens among the debris, footing treacherous, both of you stumbling as much as fighting, blade and claw finding each other more by instinct than sight in the smoke-thick dark.

He catches you against a half-standing wall at one point, cold spreading fast through your chest, and for a moment the world narrows down to just that cold and the crumbling brick at your back. You shove off the wall with everything you have rather than let it pin you, and the sudden movement catches him off guard.

You use that half second to circle rather than retreat, putting a fallen beam between you and forcing him to come around it, slower than he would like. When he finally does, impatient now, you are already set and waiting, and the blade finds him clean as he commits to the last few steps.

He goes down among the ash and blackened wood, unravelling into fog indistinguishable from the smoke still hanging over the block, and by the time it clears you are alone again in the ruin, breathing hard, the only living thing left standing in a street that has already lost everything else once tonight. You pick your way back out over the collapsed beam slowly, ash settling behind you, glad the fire brigade had already given up before they saw what came after.

The clocktower gears have not turned in years, frozen mid-motion by rust and neglect, and you have to climb through them rather than around them to reach the small platform where the Vampire Umbral Lord waits, perfectly balanced on a stopped gear the size of a cart wheel. He tells you that time stopped being his enemy the moment it stopped moving at all, up here.

He steps off the gear the instant you clear the last climb, crossing the narrow platform in the space of a breath, and the fight that follows happens entirely among frozen machinery, both of you ducking under stopped arms and around toothed wheels taller than either of you. It is close, cramped fighting, no room for a wasted swing.

He gets a claw through your guard once, catching your side, cold blooming outward fast enough to steal the breath from your lungs, and you have to grab a gear tooth just to keep your footing on the narrow walkway. You use the grip to swing yourself around rather than simply catching yourself, turning the stumble into an attack.

It lands better than planned, catching him against the same stopped gear he had been balanced on a moment before, and he staggers back into it hard enough that something deep in the tower's works groans, as if it remembers, faintly, how to move.

The finishing blow drives him back further still, and he goes down between two frozen gears, unravelling into thin fog that drifts up through the tower's broken face and out over the rooftops, and somewhere far below, for just one confused moment, the old clock strikes an hour it has not struck in years. You climb back down through the stopped machinery slower than you climbed up, letting your heartbeat settle to match the silence the tower has already returned to.

The opera house has been dark for a decade, its marquee letters fallen one by one until the name it once advertised is unreadable, but tonight the stage inside is lit, candles arranged in a perfect circle, and the Vampire Umbral Lord stands center stage like he has been waiting for an audience that never quite arrives. He tells you every performance deserves a proper ending, and that yours is overdue.

You climb up onto the stage from the empty seats, dust rising around your boots, and he crosses the boards to meet you with a theatrical flourish that turns lethal the instant his claws are in range. The first exchange happens right there under the candlelight, shadows thrown huge and wrong against the curtain behind you both.

He gets in close during the second exchange, cold spreading through your side fast enough to buckle one knee, and for a moment you are sure the show is about to end badly for you rather than for him. You catch yourself on the edge of the orchestra pit, barely, and use the recovery to put a music stand between the two of you.

He knocks the stand aside easily, but the half second it costs him is enough, and you come in under his reach while he is still finishing that gesture, blade finding true where his coat has gone thin at the shoulder. He staggers back toward the candles, coat smoking faintly at the tear.

The last blow sends him down among the candles themselves, and he unravels into fog that drifts up toward the ruined chandelier overhead, gone before it reaches the highest balcony, and the candles burn on alone now, lighting an empty stage for an audience that finally, mercifully, never has to show up. You climb back down into the dusty seats before you leave, taking one last look at the stage, and blow out the candles yourself on your way to the door.

The old mint has not struck a coin in fifty years, its furnaces cold, its presses rusted still, but the air inside is warm anyway when you push through the door, warm the way a held breath is warm, and the Vampire Umbral Lord stands beside the largest press like he owns the building's memory of its own purpose. He tells you that this city forgot how to make anything worth having long before he arrived.

He does not wait for you to answer. He crosses the floor between the silent machines faster than the space should allow, and the first exchange happens right among the rusted presses, narrow aisles forcing both of you into close, ugly fighting with no room to retreat from a bad angle.

He catches your wrist at one point, cold flooding up your arm fast enough that your fingers go numb around the hilt, and for a terrifying half second you are sure you are about to lose the blade entirely. You twist free rather than pull straight back, and the awkward angle saves you, blade staying in your grip by inches.

You use the momentum of the twist to come around into a strike rather than simply recovering your stance, and it catches him square as he moves to press the advantage, driving him back into one of the silent furnaces hard enough that old ash sifts down from somewhere above.

He does not rise from the furnace floor. He unravels there among fifty years of cold ash, fog drifting up through the broken flue and out into the night sky, and the old mint goes quiet again, properly quiet this time, the kind of silence a building settles into once it is finally done being haunted. You leave the way you came, past the silent presses, and the door swings shut behind you on a building that will not warm again tonight.

The apothecary shelves are still fully stocked, jars of powders and tinctures nobody has touched in years, dust settling thick over labels that promised cures for illnesses the city has long since forgotten. The Vampire Umbral Lord sits behind the counter as if he were the shopkeeper, and he tells you that every remedy in this room is useless against what actually ails this city, and that only he understands the real cure.

You do not stay to hear more of his philosophy. He comes around the counter fast, knocking jars aside as he moves, glass shattering and powders scattering into small glowing clouds that catch what light comes through the shuttered windows. The fight happens right there among the wreckage of a hundred spilled remedies, footing slick with broken glass and spilled tincture.

He catches you across the jaw with the back of one clawed hand, not his usual cold reach but a simple brutal blow, and it rattles you enough that you stumble into a shelf, more jars raining down around your shoulders. You come up swinging anyway, half blind from the powder cloud, trusting instinct over sight.

It works better than you expect. The blade catches him across the ribs as he steps forward to press his advantage, and he recoils with a hiss, more surprised than truly hurt, backing into the counter he had been so comfortable behind a minute earlier.

You do not let him find his footing again. The last strike drives him back over the counter entirely, and he unravels there among his own useless remedies, fog rising to mix with the last of the scattered powder clouds, gone before either color settles back down onto the apothecary's ruined floor. You leave the shelves as they fell, powders drifting slowly to rest, and pull the shuttered door closed behind you on your way back out to the street.

The old bathhouse has been dry for decades, its pools cracked and empty, its tiles cold underfoot, but steam rises anyway from somewhere in the deepest chamber, and that is where you find the Vampire Umbral Lord waiting, seated at the edge of a pool that should hold no water and somehow holds fog instead. He tells you that this city used to come here to be clean, and that he finds the irony delicious now.

He rises as you approach, coat dripping something that is not quite water, and crosses the cracked tile floor faster than his casual posture suggested he could move. The first exchange happens right at the pool's edge, both of you fighting for footing on tile slick with condensation, steam curling up around your ankles.

He shoves you toward the empty pool at one point, and you catch the edge just before going over, tile crumbling slightly under your grip, cold fog from the pool below rising up around your dangling legs like it wants to pull you the rest of the way down. You haul yourself back up through sheer stubborn refusal to end the fight that way.

That refusal costs him. He has closed in to finish the job, confident, and you are already swinging by the time he realizes you have found your footing again, blade catching him across the side before he can retreat back out of range.

He staggers into the empty pool himself this time, and the fog rises up to meet him rather than the other way around, swallowing him whole in steam that dissipates a moment later leaving nothing behind but damp tile and a coat, already growing cold. You stand at the pool's cracked edge a while longer, watching the last of the steam curl up toward the ceiling, before you finally turn and pick your way back out through the dry, silent halls.

The tannery has been abandoned since the guild that ran it collapsed, its vats gone dry, its hooks still hanging empty from rafters thick with cobweb, and the smell that lingers is old enough that it has stopped being unpleasant and become simply part of the building. The Vampire Umbral Lord stands among the hanging hooks like they were put there to frame him, and he tells you that crime built half this city and that he only ever collected what crime already owed him.

He steps between two hooks as you approach, coat brushing them without a sound, and the fight starts the moment you are close enough for either of you to reach the other, claws and blade tangling among the hanging chains in a fight that has no clean room to retreat through. Every step back risks catching a hook in the shoulder.

He forces you against the rafters at one point, cold spreading fast through your arm, and you feel a hook press cold and sharp against your back, a second danger you cannot afford to forget even mid fight. You duck sideways rather than straight back, letting the hook take a strip of your coat instead of your skin.

The duck buys you an angle he did not expect, and you use it without hesitation, driving in low while he is still tracking where you should have been standing. The blade finds him clean, and he staggers back into the hanging hooks himself, one catching his own coat and holding him upright a beat longer than he wants.

That beat is enough. The finishing strike lands before he can free himself, and he unravels right there among the chains, fog drifting up into the cobwebbed rafters and out through a gap in the roof, gone, and the hooks sway gently afterward, empty again, the way they were before he ever claimed this place.

The wine cellar under the ruined tavern goes deeper than any cellar should, barrels stacked floor to ceiling in rows that seem to multiply the longer you walk between them, and at the very back, past the last barrel, the Vampire Umbral Lord waits with a cup he never drinks from. He tells you that this cellar has kept its secrets for a century, and that you are the first to come looking who was worth keeping.

He sets the cup down without spilling a drop and crosses the narrow aisle between barrels faster than the cramped space should allow, and the fight that follows happens entirely among the stacks, barrels splintering under stray blows, wine and something darker than wine mixing together on the stone floor beneath your boots.

He gets you pinned against a barrel at one point, cold spreading through your chest, and the barrel splits behind you under the pressure, sending you both stumbling sideways in a splash of old wine that reeks of something gone wrong long ago. You use the stumble to twist free rather than fight the fall.

The twist puts you at an angle he clearly did not expect, blade already rising as he turns to follow you, and it catches him hard across the shoulder before he can adjust his footing on the wine-slick stone. He recoils into another stack, barrels toppling around him in a slow, splintering avalanche.

The last strike finds him before he can climb free of the wreckage, and he unravels among the broken barrels, fog rising through the gaps between them and drifting toward the stairs, gone before it reaches the door, leaving only spilled wine and splinters behind in the deep, quiet dark. You climb back up out of the cellar slowly, wine soaking into your boots, glad to trade the deep old dark for whatever ordinary night is waiting above the tavern's ruined floor.

The dye works has stood empty since the last guild abandoned it, vats gone dry and cracked, but color still clings stubbornly to every surface, walls stained deep blue and red from decades of work nobody remembers doing. The Vampire Umbral Lord waits among the stained vats, coat catching the last of the dye's color like it belongs there, and he tells you that this city colored the whole world once, before it forgot how to make anything but debt.

He comes at you between the vats fast, faster than the cramped space between them should let him move, and the fight opens close and brutal, blade and claw finding each other in narrow gaps meant for workers carrying buckets, not for anyone swinging steel. Dried dye crumbles off the walls with every impact, dusting the air a strange soft color.

He drives you back against a cracked vat at one point, cold spreading through your shoulder, and the vat gives way behind you under the combined weight of the blow, sending you both stumbling through a cloud of old dried dye that stains everything it touches for a moment before settling. You use the confusion of the cloud rather than fighting through it.

You strike blind, trusting the sound of his movement over sight, and it connects harder than you expected, catching him solidly as he emerges from the dye cloud still disoriented himself. He staggers back into another vat, this one holding out just long enough for him to catch himself against it.

The finishing blow does not give him that chance twice. He goes down among the stained vats, unravelling into fog tinted faintly by all the old color still clinging to the air, and it drifts up and out through a hole in the roof, gone, leaving the dye works exactly as empty and forgotten as it was before he moved in.

The tollhouse at the old bridge has collected no coin in years, its gate rusted open, its ledger books swollen with damp and mold, but a fire burns in the hearth anyway when you push inside, and the Vampire Umbral Lord sits beside it reading one of the ruined ledgers like the numbers still matter to him. He tells you every crossing this city ever made owed a fee, and that most of those fees were never properly collected.

He sets the ledger down without marking his place and rises to meet you before you have crossed half the room, faster than his unhurried reading suggested he could move. The first exchange happens right at the hearth's edge, firelight throwing huge shifting shadows across walls stained with old damp and older mold.

He shoves you toward the fire itself at one point, close enough that the heat licks at your sleeve, and you have to twist hard to avoid going straight into the flames, the movement costing you your footing entirely for one bad half second. You catch yourself on the mantel instead of the floor, barely.

The catch turns into a shove of your own, using the mantel for leverage rather than just balance, and you come off it faster than he expects, blade already swinging as you close the distance he thought he still had. It catches him clean across the chest, deeper than any hit before it in this fight.

He staggers back from the hearth, coat smoking where the fire caught its hem, and the last blow finishes what the flame started, sending him down into fog that the fire's draft pulls straight up the chimney and out into a sky that has never once, in all its years, bothered to keep an honest count of anything that left this city that way. You sit by the fire a while afterward, letting your breathing settle, before you leave the ruined ledgers exactly where they lay and cross the old bridge without paying anyone a toll at all.

The old customs house sits right at the harbor's edge, its scales still hanging from the ceiling though nothing has been weighed on them in years, chains rusted, pans tilted crooked and empty. The Vampire Umbral Lord stands beneath the largest scale as if it were a crown suspended over him, and he tells you that every ship that ever docked here paid twice, once to the harbor and once, quietly, to him.

He steps out from beneath the scales the moment you are close enough to matter, crossing the wide stone floor faster than the distance should allow, and the fight opens right there under the hanging chains, both of you ducking beneath rusted pans as you trade blows across the customs floor. Every miscalculated step risks a faceful of ancient rust.

He catches your leg with a low sweep at one point, cold flooding down through your knee fast enough to buckle it entirely, and you go down hard on the stone, blade skidding a few feet away from your open hand. You do not panic. You roll toward it rather than away, closing the distance rather than losing it.

Your fingers close around the hilt just as he closes in to finish what the sweep started, and you come up swinging rather than standing, catching him low and unexpected as he commits to the final step. He staggers back into the hanging scales themselves, chains rattling loud across the empty floor.

The last blow catches him tangled in his own chains, no room left to dodge, and he unravels there beneath the crooked pans, fog rising up through the broken customs house roof and out over the harbor, gone before the tide even finishes coming in, leaving the old scales swinging gently, empty, honest again at last. You stay a moment longer, watching the pans settle level for the first time in years, and then walk back out onto the docks where the tide is still, patiently, coming in.
